question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why nursing support will not be provided to summer schemes for children with special educational needs in summer 2026.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) salary; (ii) pension; and (iii) holiday entitlements, for classroom assistants on (a) permanent 12 month contracts; (b) permanent term time only contracts; and (c) temporary contracts
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the salary, pension and holiday entitlements for Classroom Assistants are determined in line with nationally negotiated NJC terms and conditions. The key points are outlined below.(i) SalaryClassroom Assistants are paid according to the NJC pay spine. Relevant pay points are:General Classroom Assistant: SCP 5–8: £25,583 - £26,824Classroom Assistant (SEN): SCP 11–14: £28,142 - £29,540Classroom Assistant (Additional SEN): SCP 17–20: £31,022 - £32,597The most recent circular confirms revised pay rates effective 1 April 2025, which apply equally to (a) permanent 12-month, (b) permanent term-time, and (c) temporary staff.(ii) PensionAll categories of Classroom Assistant are equally eligible to participate in the Local Government Pension Scheme (LGPS).  Pension contributions are determined by the employee’s weekly wage, which determine the contribution band as detailed in the pension banding document.Employer contributions are also applied in accordance with LGPS requirements as outlined in the Employer contribution rates document.  Rates therefore vary depending on salary point and working hours but are not determined by contract type whether (a) permanent 12-month, (b) permanent term-time, or (c) temporary staff.(iii) Holiday EntitlementHoliday entitlements are set out in JNC Circular 140 and apply equally across (a) permanent 12-month contracts, (b) permanent term time only contracts; and (c) temporary contracts.The applicable details are as follows:23 days annual leave, increasing with length of service to 29 days after 5 years.Entitlements are calculated on a 5-day working pattern, with adjustments made for alternative working patterns.Term-time only staff receive leave pro-rata, based on working 10 months of the year.Part-time staff also receive leave on a pro-rata basis.All Classroom Assistants receive 12 statutory holidays, also applied pro-rata where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the timeline of when his Department first became aware of the presence of asbestos at the Meridi Street site; and (ii) any actions that were taken at each stage.
answer_text: In 2011 NIEA became aware of the presence of asbestos-containing material (ACM) at the Meridi Street site. Following investigation, individuals were prosecuted, received suspended prison sentences in January 2014. During the same period, Belfast City Council undertook certain remedial works at the site. In 2018, NIEA received another report about asbestos waste at the site. The area was then secured to keep the public from accessing it. In May 2025 NIEA was made aware that ACM remained on the site and that the site had been breached. Given the circumstances between May and July 2025, it was not possible to eliminate the risk entirely as the site could not be safely cleared to allow removal of the ACM. NIEA therefore took all practical actions available to mitigate the risk of asbestos fibre release and to protect public health. Following the bonfire in July 2025, NIEA served a notice on the landowner under Article 27 of the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 (WCLO) directing the remediation of ACM from the site. After remedial work, NIEA engaged an independent environmental consultant to assess the site for ground-level ACM. The consultant’s report confirms ACM fragments persist on the site. While risk is minimal if the site stays secure, it increases with unauthorised access or disturbance. The site remains private property and has been secured with fencing and warning signage. NIEA, in conjunction with partner agencies, continues to advise that unauthorised persons should not access the site. Given the privately owned status of the land, responsibility for removal of hazardous material rests with the landowner. NIEA has also commenced a criminal investigation into potential breaches of the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, which remains ongoing. Therefore, no further comment can be made about this action as this may prejudice the ongoing investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of cases involving serious violence against women that are currently awaiting trial, broken down by length of delay.
answer_text: Information held on management information systems indicate that, at 1 March 2026, there were 1,126 cases where the main offence was a Violence Against the Person (VAP) offence or a sexual offence, where the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) had made a decision to prosecute but where the first court date after that decision had not yet occurred.  However, the Department does not hold information on the gender of a victim as part of its live case dataset, apart from where that is specified in an offence description, so the figure quoted includes cases where the victim is male, rather than only female, as specified in the question.  Additionally, these cases relate solely to cases where the main offence was a VAP or sexual offence.  Cases which included a VAP or sexual offence, but where this was not the main offence in a case, have not been included.To identify the number of cases involving serious violence against women that are currently awaiting trial would require a manual search of case records by the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS), which, given the number of prospective cases involved would be cost prohibitive.Note:The figure quoted is sourced from information held on the Causeway Management Information System. The figure quoted relates to cases where the main offence is a VAP or sexual offence.  The figure relates to cases where there has been a decision to prosecute by PPS but where the first court date after that decision had not yet been reached, though that first court date is not necessarily a first date of trial. Because of the stage these cases are at, there is no indicator as to whether they would end up being dealt with in magistrates’ courts or in Crown Court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidance that has been issued to schools, colleges and universities on meningitis prevention and early identification.
answer_text: The PHA has issued public health information to parents, guardians and staff of a school with a suspected case last week, which has since been confirmed negative for bacterial meningitis.  The public have been urged to remain vigilant for the signs and symptoms of meningitis and to act fast if they suspect they may be symptomatic.  Further information is available at: PHA statement - meningitis | HSC Public Health Agency and on NI Direct Meningitis | nidirectThe PHA also met with officials from the Department of Education, the Education Authority and the two NI universities last week to discuss the outbreak in Kent and to provide public health information. The PHA will be closely monitoring the situation regarding NI students returning home from university in England in the coming weeks and providing further public health advice as required. The risk to the NI public is currently low.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered a catch-up or booster campaign for teenagers and young adults, particularly those entering further and higher education.
answer_text: The Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI), an independent, expert scientific advisory committee, advises the four UK Health Departments on all vaccination and immunisation matters.  At present, JCVI has not recommended any additional vaccine catch-up or booster campaign for teenagers or young adults.  JCVI is currently reviewing evidence following a recent outbreak of meningitis B in Kent and my Department will consider any updated JCVI advice once it becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to increase public awareness of meningitis symptoms, particularly among parents and young people.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency has written to HSC Trusts, Primary Care GPs, Community Pharmacy and GP Out of Hours providers to provide an update on the outbreak in England, and to outline actions required of HSC services in NI.  The Agency has also provided public health advice directly to schools, university and education bodies and provided public facing advice and information, including on symptoms, on its website at: PHA statement - meningitis | HSC Public Health Agency and on NI Direct at Meningitis | nidirect

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether contractors carrying out pothole repairs on behalf of her Department are required to provide a warranty or guarantee on the repairs undertaken.
answer_text: Contractors carrying out pothole repairs on behalf of my Department are subject to contractual quality and durability requirements rather than a general warranty or guarantee.Temporary and permanent pothole repairs are mainly delivered through Term Contracts for SmallScale and Responsive Works. The quality and durability requirements for these repairs are set out in the Specification for Highway Works, alongside contractspecific amendments. Durability is ensured through compliance with the specification, including the use of approved materials meeting relevant Standards, adherence to defined construction and workmanship requirements, and the application of inspection and testing regimes. Temporary repairs are intended to address immediate safety hazards until a permanent repair is completed. They are not designed to provide longterm durability, and the contract does not specify related time periods. Contractors must, however, ensure that temporary repairs remain safe and effective for their shortterm purpose. Permanent repairs are covered by a 52week defect correction period, during which contractors must rectify any defects arising from their materials or workmanship. In addition, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed, and issues will also be recorded at the next routine inspection.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 39643/22-27, as her Department does not track the methods used by contractors to fill potholes, how it monitors the quality and cost effectiveness of methods used for patching in each case.
answer_text: As stated in my previous written response, the materials, specifications, quantities and methods used for carriageway repairs vary from location to location and costs are attributed to my Department’s budget for patching.Therefore, it is not possible to provide a breakdown of the repairs undertaken using each method when the total number of repairs is considered. The process of checking repairs forms part of a quality regime involving the identification of a number of repairs, including location, date of repairs and details of repair instructed. This information allows for repair quality to be checked in line with the contractual requirements.This process is markedly different to undertaking a review to determine the number and proportion of repairs using each method, over each of the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the steps his Department is taking to reduce delays in allocating disability social workers to families with disabled children.
answer_text: My Department recognises the significant impact that delays in allocating disability social workers can have on children with complex needs and on the families who rely on timely support. Ensuring that these delays are minimised, and that children with disabilities receive the right help at the right time, remains a clear priority for the Department and for our Trusts.Like many parts of the health and social care system, Children with Disability teams continue to face challenges in recruiting and retaining qualified social workers. This has created capacity pressures which, in turn, affect the speed at which cases can be allocated. To address this, the Department has directed recent ministerial investment towards increasing the capacity of Trust social work teams, supporting targeted recruitment, and introducing a broader skills mix so that appropriately qualified workers can take on key tasks and free up social workers to focus on statutory responsibilities. These measures are intended to directly support more timely allocation of cases.All Trusts are also required to follow the regional Guidance for the Management and Monitoring of Unallocated Social Work Cases. This ensures that Children with Disability cases awaiting allocation are consistently risk assessed, recorded, and actively reviewed, with the most urgent cases prioritised in a transparent and equitable way.My Department monitors workforce vacancy levels and unallocated cases through detailed monthly performance reports from each Trust. Where pressures are identified, we work closely with Trusts to agree and oversee improvement actions. Through increased investment, strengthened oversight, and a focus on workforce stability, the Department is taking clear steps to reduce delays and improve the timeliness of social work allocation for families with disabled children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any formal support structures that are in place to assist families during cancer treatment, including how families are made aware of these services.
answer_text: Formal support structures within cancer services are in place to ensure that children, young people, and families affected by cancer are fully informed and supported throughout the care pathway. A Clinical Nurse Specialist (CNS) is allocated to patients diagnosed with cancer, who act as the key worker for both the patient and their family. The CNS provides guidance, and makes referrals to age-appropriate and relevant services.  They will also sign post or refer patients and their families to the local Macmillan Information and Support Centre, which offers a wide range of support on all aspects of living with cancer.  This may include emotional support, practical information and advice, and signposting to age-appropriate counselling, peer support, and specialist resources. As part of standard practice, CNSs complete a Macmillan Holistic Needs Assessment.  This assessment helps identify whether the patient has children or young people who may require additional emotional, psychological, or practical support.  Where such needs are identified, referrals are made to the most relevant and age-appropriate services.  Children and young people with their own cancer diagnosis receive care through the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. Support for these patients and their families is coordinated according to assessed need and clinical judgement.Furthermore, my Department provides an online Directory of Cancer Support Services to help families identify relevant local support services.  Cancer Focus NI also works closely with schools attended by the young people to ensure that appropriate resources are available for the school. My Department recognises the vital contribution of the voluntary and community sector and, through the Cancer Charities Grant Scheme launched in November 2025, will provide £1 million annually to enhance support for cancer patients and their families.I hope this information helps clarify the support currently available to ensure children, young people, and families affected by cancer receive the care and support they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to expand or improve support services for families and young people affected by cancer.
answer_text: In February 2025, my Department published Regional Standards of Care for Adolescent and Young Adults (AYA) with cancer in Northern Ireland.  This comprehensive framework is to ensure that adolescents and young adults (ages 13–25) diagnosed with cancer in Northern Ireland receive consistent, equitable, and developmentally appropriate care across the region. Key principles include a regional networked model of care, a multidisciplinary specialist AYA team, holistic and developmentally appropriate care addressing the medical, psychological, social and emotional needs of young people, including end of treatment support and smooth transition into adult services.The standards support implementation of the Northern Ireland Cancer Strategy and align with best practice models for teenage and young adult cancer services. They can be found at https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/regional-standards-care-adolescents-and-young-adults-cancer-northern-ireland.A regional governance structure has now been established to oversee the implementation of these standards, with clinical leadership in place within the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to drive this programme of work. This framework will enable continuous improvement through the delivery of a coordinated, regional, networked model of care.The scope and impact of this work will be strengthened through close collaboration across statutory and voluntary sectors. Importantly, the programme will be shaped by the expressed needs and voices of adolescents and young adults living with cancer, ensuring that support services for them and their families continue to expand and improve in a meaningful and responsive way.Cancer services are also piloting an in-house cancer patient experience system across several tumour sites. Subject to the successful completion of this pilot, the system will be implemented regionally to ensure that people of all ages, including children and young people, are able to provide feedback on their care. The information gathered will support and inform future service improvement for families and young people affected by cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in respect of the potential impact of the Irish Football Association's restructuring of intermediate football, and in advance of the grassroots element of the Northern Ireland Football Fund, whether he has any commitment from the Irish Football Association regarding the consistency of home-ground criteria at each level moving forward to ensure investment is future-proofed.
answer_text: The Irish FA have not made any commitment to my Department regarding the restructuring of intermediate football. However, my officials will continue to work closely with the Irish FA to ensure that any facilities upgraded through TNIFF are aligned with the standards required at each level of competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on bringing forward legislation to establish a managing authority for controlled schools.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her work on post-19 provision for school leavers with special educational needs.
answer_text: Officials have led the development of a cross-departmental bid to the Transformation Fund, focused on improving pathways and transitions for these leavers.  If successful, it will enable provision such as SEN apprenticeships and supported apprenticeships, and better transition arrangements across the Health Trusts. The Education and Training Inspectorate evaluation of the SEN arrangements in our Further Education Colleges will conclude shortly and help inform the good practice we want to build on.  Further Education College staff have been participating in bespoke training, developed by Ulster University through my Skills Fund, and there have been enhancements to the physical environment in some of our Colleges too.  I have written to Executive colleagues to drive crossgovernment action to tackle significant system gaps including the lack of Specialist Provision for 16-to-18 year olds, and highlighting the difficulties created by the Learning Disability eligibility threshold. Officials are also progressing cross-departmental work on data sharing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any programmes available to allow community groups, local councils or other organisations to apply for trees and other plants to improve the environment and biodiversity in their local communities.
answer_text: My Department provides financial assistance to landowners, including community groups and councils, to create new woodlands through the Forest Expansion Scheme and the Small Woodland Grant Scheme.		The Department provides support to the not-for-profit voluntary sector and councils currently through Strand Two of the Environment Fund to deliver key environmental outcomes, including habitat improvement. The fund is available for applications relating to urgent, emerging priority environmental projects, land purchases and projects which would lever significant match funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the number of (i) trees planted; (ii) plants planted; and (iii) acres of woodland created, as part of the Forests for Our Future programme.
answer_text: My Department supports the delivery of the Forests for Our Future programme by providing grant aid to third party landowners to create new woodlands and establishing new woodland on Forest Service land. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to address the illegal use of e-scooters.
answer_text: Following devolution of justice matters in 2010, authority for the development and introduction of criminal and civil offences and penalties was devolved to each Northern Ireland Assembly Department.It falls to the Department owning the overarching policy area, therefore, to consider whether there are gaps in the law or amendments required to existing legislation and, where that is the case, responsibility for any subsequent legislative changes remains the responsibility of that Department. The illegal use of e-scooters is a road safety policy area, and as such it is for the Department for Infrastructure to lead on this issue, including on the creation of new offences if required. The Department of Justice can, however, advise on whether any proposed offences or penalties are proportionate and consistent with existing law for Northern Ireland.With regard to e-scooters being illegally driven on the road under the current law or to other dangerous practices involving their use, the PSNI will continue to engage young people to make them aware of the dangers of such behaviours and enforce the law as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any planned proposals to introduce regulations governing the use of e-scooters.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have no plans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI. It also remains illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and spaces in Britain, unless they are being used as part of the ongoing Department for Transport (DfT) trial of rental e-scooters scheme. This scheme has been extended until May 2028, and DfT may introduce legislation to enable a more general use of e-scooters there once the pilot scheme is complete and its outcomes assessed. The Department is exploring measures to raise awareness around the dangers associated with e-scooters. The Department is also monitoring the progress of a pilot scheme for rental e-scooters in Britain, as well as the impact of the legislative changes in the south which made the use of e-scooters legal and regulated their use on public roads from May 2024. This evidence base can help inform the department of what steps to take in relation to the registration and regulation of e-scooters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of temporary patches for potholes made, in each of the last three years; and (ii) the number of those repairs that have had to be repeated, before a more permanent fix was made.
answer_text: As the target repair times varies in accordance with the severity of the defect and the traffic volume of the road, my Department does not hold the information requested in a readily accessible format. My Department always seeks to undertake a first time permanent repair within the target repair times, however it may occasionally be necessary to carry out an initial repair in temporary material in advance of planning a separate visit for a permanent repair, for example on a busy junction.  In addition, out of hours responses may require an initial repair in temporary material in advance of a permanent repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to review the speed limit on the Ballygowan Road, at and passing, Ryan Park, Belfast.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road, the Department’s policy is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads. This takes into account factors such as the characteristic of the road, the speed of existing traffic, the degree of frontage development, and road traffic collision data which is monitored using data provided by the PSNI. The Ballygowan Road is largely rural in nature with little frontage development and is subject to the national speed limit. The Department is currently carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions. A public consultation exercise was launched on 14 January 2026 and will close on 22 April 2026. I would encourage everyone to contribute to this significant and important piece of work. Details can be found at Speed limit review launched by DfI | Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about driver behaviour, such as speeding, should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any steps her Department takes to assesses its ability to defend claims for vehicle damage arising from road defects, under Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993; and (ii) her assessment of whether the prevalence of temporary pothole repairs and deferred permanent works has affected that assessment.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has a statutory duty under the Roads (NI) Order 1993 (Roads Order) to maintain public roads. However, there is no automatic entitlement to compensation and claimant/plaintiffs must first establish the road was dangerous to traffic/road users. The duty to maintain relates solely to the ‘surface’ or ‘fabric’ of the road and does not include a road verge. This does duty not mean the road must be free from all irregularities or changes in level but we must take all circumstances of the particular road and environment into account.Where a claimant/plaintiff has established a road is dangerous, the question moves to whether the statutory defence under Article 8 of the Roads Order is available – i.e. whether my Department has taken “such care as in all the circumstances was reasonably required to secure that the part of the road to which the action relates was not dangerous for traffic”.The reference to taking “such care as in all the circumstances was reasonably required” ensures that the Article 8 statutory defence recognises, inter alia, that there are finite resources available to my Department for the maintenance of roads.  In addition, it has also been judicially determined that no road maintenance regime can prevent all defects, or immediately deal with every defect. In order to make best use of the finite resources available, an inspection and repair regime has been established which is set out in my Department’s Road Maintenance Standards for Safety. This document is publicly available using this link: RSPPG E019 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).  Due to the ongoing constraints on public funding, we are operating a ‘Limited Service’, details of which are available to view using this link: Road maintenance standards for safety – limited service in 2021/2022 DEM 160/21 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).These documents set out inspection frequencies, categories of actionable defects, the related timescales for repairs, and details relating to scheduled maintenance. Road inspection frequencies are risk based and are determined by taking into consideration both traffic volumes and road class. My Department investigates and defends public liability claims, with every case turning on its own facts. When a claim is received, an investigation is undertaken to establish if my Department has met its legal duty. This includes an assessment of whether the Standards were adhered to. In addition, issues regarding the nature and timing of repairs instructed and completed will be considered. In cases, where my officials believe the Department can raise a legal defence, claims will be repudiated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) under which R-code the pothole on Ballygowan Road at Ryan Park was initially logged; and (ii) whether that initial categorisation was changed.
answer_text: The Department is aware of social media reports of several vehicles sustaining damage on a pothole on the Ballygowan Road close to Ryan Park, Belfast on Saturday evening, 31st January 2026.The Department completed a temporary repair to this pothole on Thursday 29 January 2026. Unfortunately, it appears that this initial temporary repair did not hold.The Department arranged for a further temporary repair to be undertaken at the location and this repair was completed at approximately 7.50pm on Saturday evening. On both occasions these repairs were actioned as R0.A permanent repair at the location was carried out by our external Contractor on Monday 2nd February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the different methods her Department uses for reactive pothole repairs, for example, cold lay patching, hot mix patching and spray injection patching; and (ii) the number and proportion of repairs using each method, over each of the last three years.
answer_text: My Department uses a number of methods for reactive pothole repairs, both through its internal contractor teams in Operations and Maintenance and through the use of external contractors.  Methods such as cold lay patching, hot mix patching and spray injection patching are chosen based on the best use of repair for the individual defect.  The cost for repairing potholes falls within the Department’s budget for Patching. This term is applied to small scale works necessary to keep roads in a satisfactory state of repair, including infilling potholes but also the repair of small areas affected by cracking or settlement.As the materials, specifications, quantities and methods used vary from location to location and costs are attributed to my Department’s budget for patching, it is not possible to provide a breakdown of the repairs undertaken using each method.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of potholes repaired by her Department, within each of the last three years, that have been reported for repair again within two months of having some form of repair undertaken.
answer_text: My Department identifies actionable defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system.Actionable defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor.  On completion of the repair, inspections are undertaken on a percentage of completed road defect repairs by external contractors before payments are processed. Checks on the work of the Department’s internal contractor are also undertaken.In addition to the contractual checks above, issues will also be recorded at the next routine inspection.If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.Officials use DEM 160/21 Road Maintenance Standards for Safety – Limited Service when determining actionable defects and the Manual of Contract Documents for Highways Works when determining a repair to be satisfactory. Temporary and long-term repairs, as distinct from structural resurfacing, are mainly delivered through Term Contracts for Small-Scale and Responsive Works. The quality and durability requirements for these repairs are set out in the Specification for Highway Works, alongside contract specific amendments.Durability is ensured through compliance with the specification, which requires the use of approved materials meeting relevant Standards, adherence to defined construction and workmanship requirements, and the application of inspection and testing regimes.Temporary repairs are intended to address immediate safety hazards until a permanent repair is completed. They are not designed to provide long-term durability, and the contract does not specify related time periods. Contractors must, however, ensure temporary repairs remain safe and effective for their short-term purpose.Permanent repairs are covered by a 52-week defect correction period, during which contractors must rectify any defects arising from their materials or workmanship.However, long and sustained periods of adverse weather, such as those experienced recently in the North, will have an adverse freeze thaw effect on the road network, impacting on the durability of both temporary and long term repairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) legal; (ii) safety; and (ii) risk, assessments undertaken by her Department, where known pothole defects are subject only to temporary remedial works or where permanent repairs are deferred for extended periods, including how any assessment outcomes influence decisions on repair prioritisation.
answer_text: My Department identifies defects for repair through routine highway safety inspections and through public reporting via the online Report a Fault system. Maintenance staff will continue to inspect and makesafe defects by working to address the highestpriority defects as quickly as possible, in accordance with our policy and to ensure the safety of the travelling public.In determining how pothole defects should be managed, including where only temporary remedial works are applied or where permanent repairs must be deferred, my officials use engineering judgement and the Department’s engineering policies and standards. This ensures that decisions are informed by safety considerations and riskbased assessments, with safety always our top priority.Temporary repairs are used to ensure the public remain safe and to allow the Department to target those potholes that present the most serious safety concerns. As temporary repairs are by their nature temporary, officials will instruct permanent repairs to be made on a priority basis, ensuring those defects that pose the most risk to the travelling public are repaired first.Defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor. On completion of the repair, my officials undertake a percentage check to assess the quality of the pothole repair and confirm works for payment if the repair meets the required threshold. If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.Through this framework of inspection, engineering judgement, and adherence to departmental standards, the outcomes of safety and risk assessments directly influence decisions on repair prioritisation, ensuring resources are balanced while maintaining the safety of the travelling public.My Department continues to operate in line with DEM 160/21: Road Maintenance Standards for Safety in order to comply with the Article 8 duty within the Roads Order to maintain the road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether her Department is currently fully meeting its statutory duty to maintain the public road network in a safe condition, in light of the recent escalation in pothole defects.
answer_text: I am aware that the recent storms, prolonged rainfall, ice and snow have all taken their toll on our roads, and I have been working to identify and secure funding to address the problem. I can confirm that I have established a £7.85m Winter Recovery Road Fund to allow an urgent and direct focus on repairing the surface defects which are causing the most concern.The winter period has taken a severe toll on the road network. To put the current situation in context, 49,000 defects have been recorded in the last three months alone, which is close to half the total for the whole of the previous year.Given the scale of the damage caused, it is not possible to address every issue in the short-term. However, in addition to securing the additional funding being announced today, I have also asked my officials to also explore every avenue to maximise our available workforce capacity to ensure we are doing as much as we can as quickly as possible.Maintenance staff will continue to inspect and make-safe defects by working to address the highest priority defects as fast as possible, in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.I am committed to doing all I can to improve our roads and the Winter Recovery Road Fund will allow vital repairs to the worst affected areas to be carried out in a focused way and completed as soon as possible.It will be challenging to address years of underfunding; however, I am committed to improving our roads here, which I have shown through increased funding for essential maintenance this year and through development of the new Road Maintenance Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the annual cost of compensation for vehicle damage due to road deficiencies across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I can advise that there has been an upward trend in expenditure on roads related vehicle damage compensation since the 2020/21 Financial Year.In the 2024/25 Financial Year expenditure on vehicle damage compensation for the North was just over £1m. Whereas, the five-year average expenditure since 2020/21 is around £670,000 per annum.There are a number of reasons that can be attributed to this increased expenditure including rising repair costs. However, the primary reason is the corresponding upward trend in the number of vehicle damage claims received by my Department.During the 2024/25 Financial Year, 3,927 roads related vehicle damage claims were received compared to 2,273 in 2020/21.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any upcoming planned engagements in the United States of America, including over the St. Patrick's Day period.
To ask the Minister of Finance how his Department is addressing the ongoing budgetary pressures facing the Department of Education in the delivery of the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will commission an updated assessment of the junction at Old Dundonald Road, Comber Road and Grahamsbridge Road to reflect present-day demand and road safety concerns.
answer_text: The junction of the Old Dundonald Road; Comber Road; and Grahamsbridge Road in Dundonald forms a standard priority junction in which vehicles from the side roads have to give way to the main Comber Road, and avail of gaps in the traffic in order to progress. I am aware that the junction is very busy especially at peak traffic periods.In 2015, the Department commissioned a study to consider a number of possible options to improve the junction including the provision of traffic signals. The study examined signalising the junction to varying degrees including some and all of the movements.The study concluded that major reconstruction of the junction would be required to achieve a layout that would operate within capacity. My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, this scheme did not score highlight enough for inclusion in a works programme.The Eastern Transport Plan will set the framework for making transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035 for a 5 council area including Lisburn and Castlereagh and will therefore consider transport improvements in this area.The Plan adopts a “vision-and-validate” approach, focusing on a people-centred future that connects communities, promotes equity, enhances safety and wellbeing, and supports both the natural and built environment, rather than accommodating projected car travel demand. Consequently, the vision does not include a specific objective to improve traffic progression. Instead, the plan seeks to rebalance the transport network in favour of sustainable modes such as active travel and public transport, creating a system that is not dependent on private car use by providing attractive and viable alternatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the cost of successfully defending legal actions brought against his Department during his time as Minister.
answer_text: A number of legal actions successfully defended by the department are subject to appeal and it is not possible to provide full and complete costs at this time. However, the current cost of successfully defending legal actions against my department is £119k.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on planned actions to alleviate the risk of flooding at Park Avenue, Dundonald.
answer_text: I understand the impact flooding has had on homes here especially during Storm Bert in November 2024, when extreme rainfall led to the overtopping of a nearby culvert.To help reduce the impact of flooding at Park Avenue, my Department’s Rivers Directorate will continue its inspection and maintenance regime on the Enler River and its associated drainage infrastructure. Where inspections identify a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water or drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, works are programmed accordingly. In preparation for and during periods of adverse weather, inspection frequency at this location is increased, subject to the availability of resources. You may be aware that Rivers staff were on site last Friday night removing some debris which had accumulated on the screen and was causing concern. Staff continued to inspect this screen all across the weekend, along with many others, to make sure that critical drainage infrastructure was performing effectively.I have also been advised by NI Water that they have completed remedial works to their drainage systems in this area in a bid to mitigate against further heavy rainfall impacts.In addition, Departmental officials have commenced work to update the computer model for the River Enler including the river reaches in Dundonald. It is currently expected that this essential work which will help to inform understanding of fluvial (river) flood risk in the area, including at Park Avenue, will be complete in Summer 2026.Following this, my Department will commission a feasibility study to assess the fluvial flood risk within the Dundonald significant flood risk area to investigate if there are any viable options to help reduce the impacts of river flooding. The feasibility study is expected to take approximately 12-18 months to complete.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any upcoming initiatives aimed at increasing public engagement with the work of the Assembly.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission views public engagement as an activity which is central to the work of the Assembly and is constantly looking for new ways to support the Speaker, committees and Members in outreach activities.  In early 2025, the Assembly Commission approved a three-year Public Engagement Strategy to increase awareness, improve understanding and enable participation in the Assembly’s work.Key initiatives are already in place. A Visitor Experience Team now welcomes and informs visitors to Parliament Buildings. Citizen Parliaments have been launched, including the Women’s Parliament, Older People’s Parliament and most recently the Disabled People’s Parliament. Outreach continues across constituencies and at major events such as Mela and the Balmoral Show.New school resources support the curriculum and create memorable experiences for young people, while the Youth Assembly - now in its third mandate - remains central to engagement. Committees are also supported to gather evidence from people with lived experience, strengthening scrutiny.Looking ahead, plans for 2026 include a work experience programme for schools, a Speaker’s event with Men’s Sheds, activities for International Women’s Day, an Older People’s Parliament and continued participation in Balmoral Show, Mela and Youth Assembly activities.Our Public Engagement teams are committed to inclusivity, creating pathways for those who wish to engage and those who rarely do ensuring Members can actively support these programmes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on any plans to strengthen capacity of the Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels in light of the Independent Review of the Windsor Framework by the Rt Hon Lord Murphy.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any additional measures her Department is taking, in conjunction with statutory and community partners, to address the recent escalation in anti-social behaviour and violent confrontations between young people at interface areas in East Belfast, including incidents around the City East facility, which are impacting local businesses, community organisations and services that support vulnerable groups.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent escalation of anti-social behaviour (ASB) in inner East Belfast. Departmental officials have been liaising with both the PSNI and the District Policing and Community Safety Partnership (DPCSP) in an attempt to establish the underlying causes of the recent disorder and to determine what efforts are being made to address the issue.In discussion, the PSNI has advised that they have committed additional patrols focussing on areas where ASB has been reported. They have also taken the further step of delivering letters to parents/guardians of those youths engaged in ASB in the hope that this will act as an additional deterrent.The DPCSP, who lead on the Department’s response to community safety issues at a local level has established a Tension Monitoring sub-group with a specific emphasis on tackling recent ASB and this will include reviewing current youth provision in the area. From multi-agency work over recent years involving the Department’s Interfaces Team, we are aware of the broader issue of transient youths being involved in ASB. The age profile of those involved remains a concern. The mobility of the young people and capacity to gather at short notice, facilitated by social media platforms, is not entirely new; but represents an ongoing challenge. There are some best practice examples from work in North Belfast and South and West Belfast - progressed under the auspices of the Belfast PCSP - that may help address these issues. I have asked Departmental staff to ensure that lessons learned to date are shared with colleagues in partner agencies working in the east of the city.I recognise that ASB is a continuing issue in some of our local communities, causing anxiety and suffering for residents, businesses and community organisations. We will continue to work in partnership to tackle the underlying causes of such behaviour and to develop and implement targeted interventions that aim to prevent ASB, reduce tension and enhance community safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the installation of a defibrillator at Dundonald Medical Centre, including the expected timeline for its delivery and operational use.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) leads on the implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice for defibrillators. The responsibility for the procurement, installation, and upkeep of a Public Access Defibrillator (PAD) rests with each individual GP practice. The decision on whether or not to have a PAD installed is the responsibility of the practice itself.Dundonald Medical Practice have confirmed that they have a defibrillator, and it is installed and available for use within their practice during business hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how; and (ii) where, end-of-life solar panels can be recycled or safely disposed of.
answer_text: End of life solar panels are classified as Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) and should not be disposed of alongside general household waste. Householders should contact their solar panel installer in the first instance. Under the WEEE Regulations, producers (manufacturers, sellers, and importers) are legally required to arrange for the collection, treatment, recovery and environmentally responsible takeback, recycling and disposal of solar panel waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33381/22-27, to detail (i) the number of drivers recruited in each of the last three years by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT); (ii) the current number of full time drivers employed by the BHSCT; (iii) the current number of bus drivers in training by the BHSCT; and (iv) the intended ratio of drivers to service users deemed necessary by the BHSCT to ensure the effective running of transport services.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to her Department’s Environmental Information Regulations response DFI/2025-0181, regarding the locations of mobile bus lane enforcement cameras in the greater Belfast area, to detail (i) the number of fixed penalty notices issued in the last 12 months for bus lane offences, at each of the locations listed in her Department's response; and (ii) whether her Department has identified any discrepancies in enforcement levels between those locations.
answer_text: The number of PCNs issued for Bus Lane contraventions can be downloaded at the following link https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/penalty-charge-notice-dataMy Department’s approach to bus lane enforcement is regularly reviewed to provide consistent and appropriate enforcement at locations across the greater Belfast area whilst acknowledging that enforcement levels can vary due to several factors such as: -Bus lane operational timesHistoric trends in the issuing of PCNsTraffic Attendant FeedbackThe demand, including number of requests, from Translink, the public and/or elected representatives for enforcement in the area to address inappropriate / inconsiderate bus lane infringements. My Department’s enforcement approach is flexible, and resource permitting can be adjusted accordingly to address local needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of parking fines issued by traffic wardens, in each Belfast constituency, in each of the last three years; and (ii) the assessment her Department made of the consistency of parking enforcement activity across the city.
answer_text: Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) are recorded by council areas, towns and streets. It is not possible to provide details by constituency. The number of PCNs issued for On-street, Off-street and Bus Lane contraventions by year and by town/city for the last 5-years can be accessed at the following link https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/penalty-charge-notice-dataMy Department’s approach to parking enforcement is regularly reviewed to ensure consistent and appropriate enforcement at all locations, including the greater Belfast area. The review includes consideration of the type and extent of restrictions in an area needing to be enforced; the demand, including number of requests, from the public and/or elected representatives for enforcement in the area to address inappropriate / inconsiderate parking; the type/mix of streets to be enforced e.g. residential or commercial; historic trends in the issuing of PCNs; and feedback from Traffic Attendants (TAs)TAs are trained to undertake their enforcement duties in line with the procedures outlined in the Parking Enforcement Protocol https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/parking-enforcement-protocol. The Protocol ensures consistency of approach and removes the need for TAs to apply discretion when carrying out their duties.To further support enforcement consistency, my Department performs random checks of TAs undertaking their duties and sampling of PCNs to ensure they have been issued correctly. The outcomes of these checks are discussed with the service provider to ensure performance is consistently maintained.    From the 1st April 2024, local councils have taken responsibility for the enforcement of their own off-street car parks. The Department remains responsible for the three Park & Ride locations in Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the outcomes of the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust’s 2025 recruitment campaigns for the Transport Department, including the number of drivers recruited since the commencement of the campaigns; and (ii) the number of transport drivers who have left the Trust, in each of the past three years; and (iii) any measures being taken to minimise ongoing disruption to day centre transport services caused by driver shortages.
answer_text: The Belfast HSC Trust has advised that since the start of 2025 the Trust’s Transport department has embarked on five separate recruitment campaigns, with the sixth campaign starting 16 October 2025. Within the five campaigns this year, Transport have recruited five full time bus drivers, however one has since resigned.A total of 31 bus drivers have left the Belfast HSC Trust in the last three years; 6 in 2022/2023, 7 in 2023/2024, 9 in 2024/2025 and 9 in 2025/2026 to date. Belfast HSC Trust’s transport services are addressing disruptions to day centre transport by managing resources, using contingency measures such as hiring agency staff and taxis, recruiting new drivers, managing staff sickness, communicating with stakeholders, and collaborating on inclusive employment pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any discussions she has had with her counterparts in the Irish Government in order to stem the flow of drugs from the Republic of Ireland into Northern Ireland.
answer_text: AQO 2512 22-27 - ANSWER The threat posed by those who are reportedly using the Common Travel Area as a means to smuggle drugs into the UK is something we need to be both alert and responsive to. From the outset however, I should be clear that border security and international relations is a reserved matter to the UK Government. Whilst this falls outside of my area of responsibility as Justice Minister, I am however concerned at both the resource and societal implications which arise from increasing use of this trafficking corridor.   In September, I wrote to the Home Secretary expressing my concerns and asked that she urgently engage with her Irish counterparts, seeking their cooperation and assurances that consideration is being given to whether additional measures are needed to address this issue for all our protection. Through the auspices of the Intergovernmental Agreement (IGA) on Co-operation on Criminal Justice Matters, I also have regular conversations with my Irish counterpart. Most recently, in May of this year, I represented the Executive at a bilateral meeting with Minister Jim O’Callaghan, the Irish Minister of Justice, Home Affairs and Migration. This was a productive meeting, which provided a welcome opportunity to reaffirm our shared commitment to enhance practical criminal justice cooperation on this island.  You will also be aware that on 23rd June I made a statement in the Assembly where I updated you on the existing robust mechanisms in place, through the Joint Agency Task Force, for law enforcement agencies to work collaboratively to tackle the threat posed by drugs on a cross-border basis. The Cross Border Policing Strategy acts as the overarching delivery strategy for the work of the Joint Agency Task Force.  One of their six priority areas of focus is drugs. I receive six-monthly updates on this work.  My Department also chairs The Organised Crime Taskforce (OCTF). As part of the OCTF structures, there are a number of thematic subgroups, once of which has a focus on drugs. The Drugs Subgroup provides a forum for engagement between PSNI and other stakeholders in response to drug related threats. The Common Travel Area, as you would expect, is something that remains on the groups radar.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has made any progress towards greater regulation of short-term lets.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether single males leaving Mears accommodation who have been successfully granted permission to stay in the UK by the Home Office are classed as being in priority need by the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows: “Single males leaving Mears accommodation who have been successfully granted permission to stay in the UK by the Home Office will be subject to assessment for the Priority Need criteria as is the case with all other client groups. While factors leading to the granting of refugee status may be linked to the award of Priority Need the Housing Executive will apply this test in the same manner it does to all other client groups and therefore award of Priority Need will be on the individual circumstances of each case.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any circumstances, apart from when leaving Mears accommodation, in which newcomers to Northern Ireland receive priority need points where others in the same position would not.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the NIHE Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There are no points awarded for Priority Need under the Housing Selection Scheme. Priority Need is one of the four tests within homelessness legislation in the Housing (NI) Order 1988 (as amended). This identifies the Northern Ireland Housing Executive as the agency tasked with responding to homelessness. The Order places a statutory duty on the Housing Executive to provide interim and/or permanent accommodation for certain homeless households, dependent upon investigations and assessment of their circumstances. To be “accepted” as statutorily homeless, a household must meet the four tests of: • Eligibility• Homelessness• Priority Need • IntentionalityIn respect of the test for Priority Need there are no groups for which Priority Need is automatically applied. The Housing Executive applies the test for those leaving Mears accommodation in the same manner as any other client group. Therefore, there are no circumstances for which Priority Need is automatically applied for newcomers to Northern Ireland.”

To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the recent decision to dispose of the unoccupied clinic at 331 Cregagh Road, Belfast, whether any consideration has been given to a community asset transfer.
answer_text: The former Cregagh Clinic, declared surplus in September 2022, is owned by the Belfast HSC Trust (the Trust).  Land & Property Services Central Advisory Unit, who manage the disposal process for surplus assets, circulated this asset to the ‘internal market’ in July 2023 which included central and local government bodies, as well as third sector bodies via the Community Asset Transfer (CAT) scheme, who are supported by a central or local government sponsor body.  Three expressions of interest were received under the CAT scheme: Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE), Urban Youth and Solas. NIHE and Solas withdrew their interest and Urban Youth were unable to secure the necessary funds to proceed with acquisition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any consideration has been given to developing a men’s health strategy for Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the repair works to the retaining wall at Upper Newtownards Road, Dundonald, which have been causing a partial lane closure since June 2025.
answer_text: Following the partial collapse of the retaining wall at Upper Newtownards Road, my officials secured the site and implemented temporary safety measures. These remain in place to protect the public until a permanent solution is progressed.Recent works undertaken by my Department’s Contractor include the dismantling the worst affected section of the wall and the installation of a pedestrian handrail to improve safety. Officials are now preparing to carry out investigatory works to determine the cause of the failure and inform the design of a permanent repair. I fully recognise the inconvenience these closures are causing to the local community and my officials are working to reopen the footway and carriageway as soon as it is safe to do so.

answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the supply of veterinary medicines from Great Britain to Northern Ireland is under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. As such, this does not sit within my ministerial responsibilities. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on any measures she is taking to secure the future for workers and the facilities at Spirit AeroSystems that will not transfer to Airbus, following the deal announced on 28 April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any lessons that can be learned from other jurisdictions in relation to addressing educational underachievement.
answer_text: The issue of addressing educational underachievement is very broad and there are several elements to this subject which see Departmental officials proactively engaging with colleagues across other jurisdictions in order to identify examples of best practice and to seek input around various aspects of policy development.Examples include:Improving Pupil Attendance – meeting with home nations colleagues looking at ways of addressing attendance levels at school.Extended Schools Programme – an Independent evaluation has been commissioned including an examination of best practice in England, Scotland, Wales and the Republic of Ireland.A Fair Start Programme – maximising boys potential learning hub, once launched, will feature quality assured regional, cross-jurisdiction and international research.Period Dignity - meeting with home nations colleagues to examine how best to deliver the programme in schools locally.The Four Nations Equity in Education Group. The group meets quarterly and provides a forum for information sharing and discussion regarding educational underachievement and disadvantage.

To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any steps her Department has taken to strengthen relationships between the PSNI and working class loyalist communities.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 12 – AQO 1611 22-27			ANSWERMy Department is operationally independent from the PSNI.  That said, strengthening relationships between the police and the community they serve is a shared endeavour between the Department, the NI Policing Board and the Police Service of Northern Ireland.  It is also the responsibility of every political representative. My Department funds Policing and Community Safety Partnerships in every District Council area across Northern Ireland. One of the key objectives of PCSPs is to increase confidence in policing. Working to identify community concerns and develop local solutions to address these is at the heart of the Action Planning approach used by PCSPs. To further augment the policing and community contact through PCSPs, I am also aware that the PSNI’S Strategic Community Engagement Team has hosted a number of Reference, Engagement and Listening (REaL) Events which engage all communities. The most recent REaL event took place at The Spectrum Centre on the Shankill Road on the 3rd February and was attended by the Chief Constable, Deputy Chief Constable and Senior PSNI Officers from numerous Districts. In addition, the cross-Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime invests in a number of initiatives aimed at improving relations between the PSNI and the community. This includes work delivered through the Education Authority, the Department for Communities, the PSNI and The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent flooding in Park Avenue, Dundonald, for an update on any work his Department has carried out to establish the cause and a permanent solution.
answer_text: Following a period of adverse weather and very heavy rainfall associated with Storm Bert, existing drainage infrastructure maintained by NI Water and my Department in the Moat Park / Park Avenue, Dundonald area became overwhelmed.  On the day prior to this flooding, preventative screen maintenance was undertaken on drainage infrastructure at this location by my Department’s Rivers Directorate and found to be totally clear of debris.Unfortunately, the river overflowed at this location and the volume of rainfall lead to surcharging of the existing networks which are maintained by NI Water. This combination led to homes at Moat Park / Park Avenue Dundonald being flooded.NI Water advise that the properties have previously flooded before and it has investigated this matter throughout 2024 and has carried out a range of measures to improve drainage in the area. They have said this work has proved extremely challenging due a number of issues and further excavations were carried out following the flooding to facilitate further investigations.Given the nature of flooding here, I have instructed officials to review NI Water and my Department's response to this event, and the feasibility of any viable permanent flood alleviation measures and drainage improvements at this location will be reviewed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the publication of the International Relations Strategy.
answer_text: Enhancing our reputation and relationships internationally has been and will continue to be of vital importance.  We will continue to focus on strengthening our reputation as a world-class destination to live, work, visit, study and invest.  During our first nine months, we have seen personally the level of interest and support shown by our international partners.Since the restoration of the Institutions, our officials have supported official visits from over 25 different countries.We continue to take every opportunity to show that Executive is back up and running and we are open to partnerships that support our economic growth, but also help us to deliver outcomes for our citizens. 					    International interest in all that we have to offer continues to grow – we have a reputation as one of the world's best business set-up partners and as an investment destination of choice.   We have asked officials to consider a new strategy that will support the delivery of priorities as set out in the draft Programme for Government; acknowledge emerging global challenges and opportunities and set out the optimal countries and regions for engagement to maximise delivery of our shared priorities.

answer_text: The South Eastern HSC Trust has a licence agreement for 100 spaces at the Dundonald Park & Ride overflow car park, with parking permits issued to 230 staff.The Trust continually monitors the use of the Dundonald Park & Ride bus service to and from the Ulster Hospital.  Feedback from staff and Trade Union colleagues is regularly sought to ensure that the service continues to meet the needs of staff.  The Trust has expanded the bus timetable several times since the service commenced.  The last time the service timetable was reviewed and changed was in September 2023 when it was extended to operate from 06:45am.Currently the service operates on average every 30 minutes with the last service leaving the Ulster Hospital at 21.15pm.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed park and ride facility at Tillysburn.
answer_text: My Department has plans to develop a park and ride site at Tillysburn.  The proposed site is located on the A2 Belfast to Bangor transport corridor, a key arterial route into Belfast.  It will encourage commuters to choose public transport or cycling for part of their journey. It will also integrate with a proposed new cycle route linking Victoria Park and the Coastal path known as the Sydenham Greenway.  Pre-application discussions for this site had commenced with Belfast City Council Planning in August 2021.     Whilst I am supportive of expanding our Park and Ride schemes where needed, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to under funding and austerity by the British Government.  As the capital budget for my Department in 2024/25 is less than required, available funding must be prioritised accordingly.  Whilst my officials continue to consider options for Park and Ride sites, including Tillysburn, it is likely that development on Park and Ride projects will be limited in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why the roll-out of encompass has led to incomplete data from (a) the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust; and (b) the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust in the most recent diagnostic waiting times statistical release; and (ii) when the full data sets will be available for all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The adoption of the encompass system has introduced new ways of working for clinicians, leading to changes in how some activities are recorded and measured. These changes also require updates to reporting practices, including those for diagnostic procedures. Encompass has been capturing diagnostic waiting time data for both the South Eastern and Belfast Trusts since their respective “Go Lives” and work has been undertaken to ensure the information is accurate, fully validated and meets required standards. Trust information teams and the encompass programme have been working carefully, with support from my Department, to finalise and validate these reporting products. My clear expectation is that inclusion of diagnostic waiting times for the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust and the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust is reestablished as soon as possible. I recognise that inclusion of data from both trusts in the November diagnostic waiting time statistical release is contingent on the department receiving quality assured timely information.All Trusts across the UK, that have implemented similar electronic patient record systems, have required a period of stabilisation before resuming the full range of regulatory information returns. Although similar validation will be necessary as each Trust adopts encompass, ongoing efforts in the South Eastern and Belfast Trusts will pave the way for future deployments, ensuring that remaining Trusts are well placed to meet their quarterly reporting deadlines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to reintroduce the Cross-border Healthcare Directive that allows residents of Northern Ireland to seek and pay for treatment in the private sector in the Republic of Ireland and have it reimbursed by a Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) was introduced on 30 June 2021 as part of the Elective Care Framework to help reduce NI waiting lists. The scheme was a limited 12-month administrative version of the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive (CBHD) which ceased to apply to NI following the UK’s exit from the EU. The RoIRS closed to new applications on Wednesday 21 September 2022 when the funding had been exhausted. Given the pressing demands for funding across many areas of health and social care no further funding could be assigned to this scheme.A bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted to the Department of Finance during this year’s budget setting process. The Reimbursement Scheme bid of £31million was part of a wider bid for £135million of additional funding to tackle waiting lists. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. This was in addition to a separate bid of £53million, within the wider £135million, specifically to address some 4,400 patients waiting over 2 years for orthopaedic procedures. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget which you voted for, no additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists. Instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget allocation for 2024/25 represented a 2.3% reduction in funding compared to our expenditure in 2023/24. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position. As such, there is insufficient funding at present to reinstate a cross-border reimbursement scheme. The financial pressures across the Health and Social Care (HSC) system are certainly severe and the 2024/25 budget outcome falls far short of what is needed to tackle the unacceptable waiting lists. Therefore, my Department’s focus is on supporting those on suspect cancer and time critical waiting lists.While the £121m received in the June 2024/25 monitoring round makes some contribution to reducing the deficit position facing the HSC system, a significant shortfall still remains. As some £75m of this had already been factored into our 2024-25 financial plan, the remaining £46m will reduce the HSC Trust funding shortfall to £143m with a total gap of £293m. The financial position therefore remains very challenging and work continues across the HSC system to deliver the maximum possible savings and efficiency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department plans to look at the regulation of short-term lets to ensure there is not an impact on local housing supply.
