question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of (i) the increase in vacant office space, including Grade A, in Belfast city centre; and (ii) the impact this is having on the regeneration of Belfast city centre.
answer_text: My Department has not assessed how office building vacancies in Belfast city centre impact the regeneration of the city centre.My Department continues to work with Belfast City Council and other stakeholders in delivering a range of interventions targeted at city centre regeneration where resource is available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether a feasibility study has been considered in relation to converting vacant Belfast city centre commercial premises to residential premises.
answer_text: I have not considered a feasibility study for converting vacant Belfast city centre commercial premises to residential premises.    My Department continues to work with Belfast City Council and other stakeholders to contribute to realising the city’s housing and population growth targets as set out in the Belfast Agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she is undertaking any work to address the increasing amount of vacant office space in Belfast city centre.
answer_text: The Department is aware of the general surplus of office space within the Belfast City Centre area and is keen these are brought back into use. My officials in Land and Property Service (LPS) have been assisting Belfast City Council officials on this specific issue. Belfast City Council are currently engaging with property owners, property occupiers, investors and developers to identify the specific challenges and barriers related to bringing upper floors in the City Centre into use again in a residential capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an up to date assessment of the number of vacant Grade A, Grade B and Grade C sites in Belfast city centre.
answer_text: Land and Property Services does not hold data on Grade A, B and C offices in Belfast city centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on current work to enhance and regenerate Belfast city centre
answer_text: My Department provides a range of direct and indirect support to enhance and improve the physical, economic, and social appeal of our town and city centres. In addition to the extra support for town and city centres that was provided in response to Covid-19, the Department has been and continues to deliver several programmes which are designed to support the long-term viability and vitality of town and city centres. These include Public Realm and Environmental Improvements, Revitalisation Schemes, Comprehensive Development Schemes, Urban Development Grants, Village Catalyst Grants and support for the development of Business Improvement Districts.  This represents an ongoing and significant investment in our town centres which has been in place for many years. The Department will continue to deliver these programmes subject to resources being available.  The Department also helps facilitate the appeal of towns centres through legislation, such as that for Pavement Cafes and the management of event spaces.Recently the Department has supported a new strategic approach to the regeneration of town and city centres through the work of The Ministerial Advisory Group for Architecture and the Built Environment (MAG) who have created a new model for our city, town and village centres in the future through the co-production of the Living High Street Craftkit. This includes a series of tools that can facilitate engagement with communities (businesses, residents, services etc.) to co-produce, place specific Action and Delivery Plans with visions and sustainable solutions for our “Living High Streets”.My Department continues to collaborate with Belfast City Council, the Department for Infrastructure and other key city centre stakeholders to bring forward a number of strategic projects aligned with existing regeneration policy, the Belfast City Centre Regeneration and Investment Strategy, and Belfast City Council’s Belfast Agenda.These are: Public Realm projects including Belfast Streets Ahead Phase 5, Blackstaff Square, the ‘Five Cs’ and the Shankill Gateway Phase 1 (taking account of the Department for Infrastructure Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 and Eastern Transport Plan 2035). Collaborating with Belfast City Council and the NI Housing Executive to bring forward housing led regeneration schemes on a number of city centre sites: the Inner Northwest Northern Cluster (DfC lands at Kent St and Library St, and BCC lands at Little Donegall St); the Inner Northwest Southern Cluster (DfC lands at Laws Court and Samuel St; BCC lands at Smithfield Market & Car Park; and a CPS Car Park at Millfield which DfC is seeking to acquire); and DfC lands at Stewart Street.  The Department is also progressing the Queen’s Quay project. This is a major waterfront Belfast city centre development opportunity consisting of 13,000 metres² of prime development land and offering the potential development of over 50,000m² of commercial space, including residential, Grade A workspace

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will commission a review of the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider the potential of providing support for work travel expenses for those on Universal Credit to encourage the taking up of employment.
answer_text: My Department currently provides Universal Credit claimants with support for work related travel expenses to encourage the uptake of employment opportunities through our Adviser Discretion Fund (ADF). The ADF is a non-repayable grant award of up to £1500 (within a 12-month period) available through a Work Coach in one of our 35 local Jobs and Benefits offices. The fund can provide help with the cost of travelling to work or to a job interview. To apply for the ADF claimants can arrange an appointment with their Work Coach via their online journal or by contacting the Universal Credit by telephone on 0800 012 1331.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any work has been undertaken to assess the number of vacant church premises, including their current state of repair.
answer_text: My department supports Ulster Architectural Heritage to maintain a register of Heritage at Risk in Northern Ireland (HARNI). This includes listed buildings currently in use and church buildings formerly in use. Unlisted buildings in Conservation Areas are also considered. This register is not based upon a systematic survey and therefore may not have captured all such buildings in NI. As part of the HARNI review overall condition is assessed, relating to vacancy, security and condition. ‘Risk’ is allocated at grades ‘A’ – ‘F’ (‘A’ being most at risk) as an indicator of a building’s current state of repair at the time of survey.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider developing a strategy to support the regeneration of church buildings, to encourage their original purpose or for similar community purposes.
answer_text: My department is not currently considering the development of a specific strategy to support the regeneration of church buildings. 		However, it is currently developing a Heritage at Risk strategy. This will seek to prevent vacancy and encourage the regeneration, adaptation and re-use of historic buildings at risk, including church buildings.		Since 2017 the department has also supported the National Churches Trust to work with churches to develop ways to ensure that the traditional use of these buildings can be sustained.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of vacant church buildings in Belfast, broken down by listed status.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information.It does, however, hold information on vacant listed buildings or unlisted buildings in Conservation Areas and I attach information on these for Belfast. This data has been taken from Heritage at Risk register for Northern Ireland (HARNI). My department supports Ulster Architectural Heritage to maintain this register. As it is not based upon a systematic survey, it may not have captured all such buildings in Belfast.  I have arranged for a table showing vacant churches and former church buildings on the HARNI register, Belfast to be placed in the Assembly Library (Annex A).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the work he is undertaking with other agencies and public sector bodies to improve the maintenance of grass areas in local communities.
To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what plans he has to invest in pitches for grassroots and community football, to meet the growing demand.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the work her Department is carrying out to ensure universities and further education colleges work together to offer qualifications that are not competitive but complementary to each other.

To ask the Minister of Justice what action is being taken to improve communications with users of the justice system to (i) guide them through the process; and (ii) to keep them updated on their cases.
answer_text: I acknowledge the importance of providing victims of crime with appropriate and timely information, advice and support.  The Victim and Witness Charters set out the entitlements and services that victims and witnesses of crime in Northern Ireland can expect to receive from criminal justice organisations such as the PSNI and PPS. This includes their right to receive updates on their case.   In February, and in partnership with the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime, I launched a public information campaign to promote awareness of the Victim Charter. In addition to a television campaign, this work has included targeted information events across Northern Ireland  and remains ongoing. My Department is also leading multi-agency work to develop and deliver a new digital information hub for victims of crime called ‘My Justice Journey’.  The aim of the new information hub is to provide victims and witnesses with information on the criminal justice process in an intuitive and easy to read  and easy to navigate format. It will explain roles and responsibilities of criminal justice organisations and will flag where specific help and support can be found. In doing so it is intended to address fundamental concerns that have been raised by victims and their representatives about the complexity of the criminal justice system and their lack of understanding of the process and terminology. I anticipate the new information website going live later this year.In addition, I understand that PSNI has initiated similar work to develop an interactive online portal which will allow victims to track the progress of the police investigation. I understand that this work is in the early stages of development. The PSNI and my officials will remain in contact as this work develops to ensure a joined-up approach.My Department also funds Victim Support Northern Ireland and NSPCC’s Young Witness Service. Both organisations provide support and advice to victims of crime that can help to guide them through the criminal justice process.In addition to the services that are funded by my Department, the Victim and Witness Care Unit (VWCU) within PPS provides victims of crime with a single point of contact from the point that an investigation or charge file is submitted to the PPS, through to and including the outcome of any court proceedings. VWCU Case Officers keep victims updated on their case as well as referring victims for specific supports provided by other organisations.Specific supports are also in place for victims of domestic and sexual abuse, including through the ASSIST NI advocacy service which provides guidance, support and information and is tailored to individual needs; and through the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser Scheme, which provides complainants in serious sexual offence cases with publicly funded, pre-trial legal advice in respect of their case.The Probation Board for Northern Ireland’s Victim Information Unit also works to inform victims about their righ

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she is planning any reform of legal aid.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is currently undertaking two reviews of legal aid provision. His Honour Tom Burgess is currently undertaking a fundamental review of criminal legal aid which commenced on 1 October 2023. Judge Burgess is considering the criminal landscape, existing processes, levels of remuneration, future review mechanisms and is expected to report his findings and recommendations by the end of June 2024.  The scope of the review includes legal aid remuneration for advice and assistance provided at police stations, as well as representation at Magistrates’ Court, Crown Court and at parole hearings. A foundational review of civil legal services is also ongoing.  The aim of this review is to examine currently funded civil legal services and to identify and analyse opportunities for reform. The Department launched the following Calls for Evidence from 22 April 2024 to 18 June 2024:Call for Evidence for Legal Practitioners (Solicitors, Barristers, Judiciary)Call for Evidence for Civil SocietyCall for Evidence for Children and Young PeopleThese Calls for Evidence are intended to supplement the evidence and issues raised through ongoing stakeholder engagement meetings.  This will be used as part of the evidence base for recommendations for reform which will be presented for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the digitisation of the health service.
answer_text: Information has been held in digital medical records for many years. Clinicians require this information to provide all patients and service users with safe and appropriate care. Currently the encompass programme is deploying a regional Electronic Patient Record for use by Acute and Community Care services. The deployment phase is expected to conclude in Spring 2025. This will replace many, older individual systems currently in use. Primary Care providers have their own computer solution, tailored to their practice needs. There are currently 3 different commercial solutions in use.  The EPIC software suite, currently being deployed by the encompass programme to provide the Electronic Patient Record includes a patient portal, known in Northern Ireland as MyCare. This may be accessed via a mobile phone app, or a dedicated website. MyCare provides patients with personalised and secure online access to specific parts of their medical records including medications, appointments, and some test results. Information on General Practice interactions will not be available via MyCare. The MyCare portal is an evolving system and content will change and develop based on patient experience and improving technology.GP practices have the ability to offer patients access to an online app which patients can use to book appointments or request repeat medication. The services available to the patient are controlled by the individual practice.  Although other services are available via the app these have not been commissioned and are therefore not available in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that Health and Social Care staff that receive Universal Credit will lose out of two month’s benefit payments if the 5 per cent pay rise and the one-off non-consolidated payment are split, whether he will ensure that staff on Universal Credit receive one payment.
answer_text: My Department asked Regional Payroll within the Business Services Organisation (BSO) to work with Trust HR colleagues to formulate an implementation plan for all outstanding pay awards. Following their discussions over what was feasible, the resultant solution of splitting consolidated and non-consolidated payments was acknowledged as the best way of ensuring all staff would at least receive their substantive pay award in the June pay run. Non-consolidated payments will follow in August. The alternative to this would have been to delay implementation of all elements of the awards, for all staff, until September.Any additional income may have unwelcome implications for any individual in receipt of income assessed benefits. Individuals in receipt of Universal Credit should seek advice from their work coach or contact the service centre on 0800 0121 331, as neither my Department, nor individual employers can offer advice on benefit entitlements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to give traffic attendants more powers to address obstructive pavement parking.
answer_text: During November 2023 the Department made legislation that added to the list of locations where parking wholly or partly on a footway is prohibited to include beside bus lanes, bus stops, mandatory cycle lanes and mandatory school keep clears. These can be enforced by Traffic Attendants. The Department routinely reminds drivers to ‘think before you park’ and continues to take action against vehicles that are in contravention of parking restrictions, including those that apply to footways.  On 6 December 2023 the Department released a Findings Report on the responses received to the consultation on inconsiderate pavement parking and this can be accessed using this link;https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/inconsiderate-pavement-parking-findings-report I plan to issue a formal response to the consultation exercise when I have had time to consider the Findings Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following his announcement on 30 April 2024, whether those vehicles eligible for Temporary Exemption Certificates and that have already had an MOT in 2024, will be exempt from an MOT next year.
answer_text: From 1 June 2024, private cars first registered between 1 June 2019 and 31 May 2020 or between 1 June 2017 and 31 May 2018, which have a valid MOT issued by the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) will have a one-year Temporary Exemption Certificate automatically applied from the date their current MOT certificate expires. This will allow motorists to continue to drive the vehicle legally, if it is kept in a roadworthy condition, is taxed and insured.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are in place to allow deaf drivers to avail of a short notice MOT appointment, given that it specifies that you must be able to receive a telephone call as part of the process.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is aware that some of its customers, particularly those with hearing difficulties, may not be able to use the telephone.  Customers with special needs or physical disabilities should email DVA Customer Services at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk to explain their circumstances and outline their preferred method of communication.On receipt of the relevant information the DVA will then contact the customer to arrange an urgent appointment for their vehicle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the United States-United Kingdom Small and Medium-Sized Enterprise Dialogue that took place in Belfast on 16 April 2024.
answer_text: Oral Question No 12 – AQO 364/22-27The SME Dialogue is organized by the Office of the United States Trade Representative, the Department of Commerce, and the Small Business Administration alongside the British Government’s Department for Business and Trade. The 8th dialogue took place in Belfast and updates are available directly from the organisers via their websites.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the proposed dilapidation bill.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 6803/22-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action is being taken to ensure that AI programmes, such as ChatGPT, are not misused in an education setting.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of supports to educational settings to ensure good practice in relation to online safety and safeguarding. Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools contains links to online safety resources, including the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland’s Online Safety Hub, which provides information to children, young people, parents, carers and professionals about staying safe online. The Hub also provides guidance about artificial intelligence and emerging technologies, including information about ChatGPT. The Department of Education provides funding to iNEQE Safeguarding Group for the provision of the Safer Schools Northern Ireland App, which contains safeguarding information, advice and guidance tailored for schools, pupils, parents and carers.The Education Authority and C2k will shortly be issuing guidance to all EA staff and schools in relation to Open Source AI which includes ChatGPT. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations & Assessment (CCEA) operates within the Joint Council for Qualifications guidelines; AI Use in Assessments: Protecting the Integrity of Qualifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what support his Department provides for business start-ups.
answer_text: Creating a vibrant ecosystem of business start-ups across the North is one of my top priorities.  My Department provides a suite of interventions to support entrepreneurs in the early stages of business development. Support available through Invest NI, InterTradeIreland and the Further Education Colleges includes financial assistance, advice and guidance, mentoring, signposting to wider assistance and support for business plan development. Invest NI also provides funding to a number of partner organisations that deliver a range of debt and equity funds for high potential start-ups. There is a rich network of organisations working across the North to support entrepreneurs. Key organisations working in partnership with my Department include the 11 Councils, Enterprise NI and its 28 local enterprise agencies, Women in Business, Catalyst Inc. and Ormeau Baths.   Invest NI provides a comprehensive guide to business support on www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk. Two parts of the website are particularly helpful for start-up businesses - My New Business and Starting a Business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to explore the potential of Artificial Intelligence to improve health services.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Generative AI technologies offers opportunities to improve both productivity of the existing HSC workforce and the quality of care services delivered, with benefits including the freeing up of capacity to provide services to those that need it most, in settings and ways that best meets their needs.The potential capabilities of this technology must be set against the potential risks – for instance in the areas of clinical efficacy and information governance. In these areas the Department of Health and the Health and Social Care (HSC) system needs to ensure that the tools provided are trustworthy and do not breach the privacy of patients or service users.Additionally, the wide adoption of advanced technologies like AI/GenAI require a number of underlying enablers – including good quality, codified data that describes a service users needs and historic episodes and treatments (for training purposes), the clinical safety and ethical governance structures that are required to oversee the safe introduction of these AI tools, and skills in cloud services engineering and security (recognising that the compute power required to run AI algorithms is often best provided from cloud technologies). The Department of Health and its partners in the HSC are establishing structures to develop these enablers and explore the capabilities of providers and products, with a particular focus in the first instance on medical image analysis for diagnostic purposes, an area with growing evidence to support clinical effectiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to implement an ADHD strategy.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The Department of Health Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) are committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.DoH SPPG, alongside DoH policy colleagues, are considering how a future ADHD service might be delivered to meet needs, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action being taken to help combat childhood obesity.
answer_text: The current obesity prevention strategic framework, A Fitter Future for All 2012-2022, was launched in March 2012. This takes an integrated approach to obesity prevention and contains outcomes and actions across the life course for the entire population of Northern Ireland. Work is underway to develop a replacement strategy, and a public consultation on ‘Healthy Futures – A Strategic Framework to Prevent the Harm caused by Obesity, and Improve Diets and Levels of Physical Activity in Northern Ireland’ closed on 1 March 2024. The Public Health Agency (PHA) invests in a number of obesity prevention programmes and work in partnership with statutory and non-statutory partners to promote activities that encourage healthy lifestyles that contribute to obesity prevention. 22% of children starting school in NI are overweight or obese.  In order to help address childhood overweight and obesity the PHA have developed and are funding the Early Years Obesity Prevention Programme (EYOPP) across NI. This targeted programme will address risk and protective factors for childhood obesity, adopting a holistic approach focusing on parenting skills, family lifestyle habits, diet and nutrition, physical activity, and emotional wellbeing.The PHA awarded a contract to HENRY (Health, Exercise & Nutrition for the Really Young - https://www.henry.org.uk/) in Feb 2020, to provide an early years obesity prevention training programme to Health and Social Care Trusts and SureStart staff across Northern Ireland, in line with NICE Guidance PH47. The programme aims to:equip health and early years practitioners with skills, knowledge, and confidence to tackle the issue of childhood obesity; and provide families & carers, with children aged 0-5, with a preventative group-based parent programme or a targeted 1-to-1 programme as appropriate.EYOPP coordinators and child health assistants are in post in each trust to deliver programme to families. PHA - Raise, Engage, Refer training has been delivered as part of the EYOPP training package – this informs Allied Healthcare Professionals and others working in health on how to constructively raise the issue of obesity, engage with parents/carers and refer into the HENRY programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why pharmacists are unable to commence private clinics for the Pfizer and Novavax Covid vaccines due to their unavailability in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Pfizer Covid vaccine, Comirnaty® single dose (never frozen) vial, and Novavax Covid vaccine, Nuvaxovid® dispersion for injection vial, are authorised in Northern Ireland (NI) under the EU’s Centralised Authorisation Procedure. Currently, the marketing authorisation holders (MAHs) have not made authorised compliant stock available for the NI market for these products in advance of Windsor Framework arrangements coming into force from 1 January 2025. Conversely, the MAHs who have MHRA authorisations in Great Britain (GB) for these products, have made authorised compliant stock available for the GB market. In recognition that GB stock is available and is required at this time to meet patients’ clinical needs in NI, both Nuvaxovid® dispersion for injection and Comirnaty® single dose vials are presently on the NI MHRA Authorised Route (NIMAR) list. Nuvaxovid® dispersion for injection was added to the NIMAR list on 24 January 2023 and Comirnaty® single dose vials were added 21 March 2024. The addition of these products to the NIMAR list permits the movement of GB authorised stock into NI to meet clinical needs, which may potentially include pharmacists utilising this stock through appropriate private clinics.It is important to note that vaccines procured centrally by UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) for the spring 2024 vaccination programme provided under Health and Social Care (HSC) arrangements for eligible cohorts of patients in NI have not been impacted by the commercial decisions made by these MAHs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to engage with the UK government on measures to increase home ownership locally and reduce demand on the rental market.
answer_text: My Department is represented on the Inter-Ministerial Group (IMG) for Housing, Communities and Local Government which aims to foster and maintain positive relations between the four governments and facilitate collaboration wherever possible, including on the issue of affordable housing supply.Co-Ownership remains my Department’s main delivery partner for shared ownership housing, having helped over 32,000 people to buy a home. There are currently over 10,000 people living in a Co-Ownership home.Currently, an allocation of £158 million in Financial Transactions Capital funding is approved to support over 4,400 households into home ownership over the period 2020/21 and 2024/25.I recognise that the rise in house prices, high rents in the private sector and conditions in the wider economic landscape have made it more difficult for people, particularly first-time buyers, to get on the property ladder. Shared ownership provides an opportunity to those who wish to purchase a home but have some issues due to affordability, employment or debt which mean they cannot obtain a mortgage on the open market, whilst also providing a much-needed alternative to the private rented sector: around half of Co-Ownership customers come from this sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration is being given to increasing the points awarded to victims of domestic abuse under the Housing Selection Scheme.
answer_text: Changes to the Housing Selection Scheme are underway via the implementation stage of the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations. Under the current scheme, someone who leaves their home as a result of domestic abuse can be allocated points for homelessness and primary social needs points for violence or the threat of violence. My officials are working with the Housing Executive to explore options for the future of intimidation points. I will consider the award of points via the Scheme in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department is taking to reduce fly-tipping and environmental health concerns associated with unkempt alleyways in residential areas.
answer_text: Responsibilities for illegal waste dumping are split between my Department and Councils. Through the Northern Ireland Environment Agency, my Department focuses its enforcement actions primarily on cases which are high risk to health and or the environment and large-scale waste dumping activity rather than low level ad-hoc dumping often referred to as ‘fly-tipping’, which is primarily addressed by District Councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider amending the parking enforcement protocol to allow carers to park on double yellow lines whilst undertaking caring duties.
answer_text: Vehicles specifically used by the Police, Fire and Rescue Service, and the emergency medical services are provided with an exemption under our waiting restriction legislation. The general approach is that these exemptions only apply to a vehicle attending an emergency. The legislation also makes provision for specific medical practitioners if they are attending an emergency. In non-emergency situations all vehicles are expected to adhere to the parking restrictions, which are in place for the safety and convenience of all road users.Nonetheless, if an individual is issued with a PCN and they consider that there are mitigating circumstances, such as administering emergency care, then an appeal should be submitted. If sufficient supporting evidence is provided, a PCN can be cancelled.I am satisfied there is no reason to change the current operating procedures as there is a robust process in place to enable PCNs to be challenged.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 5563/22-27, to detail what action his Department plans to take on this matter.
answer_text: Article 20 of the Waste and Contaminated Land Order (Northern Ireland) 1997 places a duty on district councils in respect of the collection of household waste and Article 21 of the same Order sets out the responsibilities for councils in respect of receptacles for household waste (bins) including the provisions a council may make in relation to the placing of the bins.Should a council determine that there is a significant problem with the placing and removal of bins in its area it would be up to that council to consider if it already has sufficient powers to remedy the problem through, for example, enhanced communications to ratepayers. If not, the council may wish to engage initially with other Northern Ireland councils to establish if this is a widespread issue and whether other councils have identified successful approaches to address the matter. If a significant problem continues to exist that cannot be resolved through existing powers, then a petition on behalf of the councils can be made to my Department to amend the Waste and Contaminated Land Order (Northern Ireland) 1997 to provide additional regulatory responsibilities on all councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department has taken to address issues raised by Health and Social Care staff in relation to the Encompass IT system.
answer_text: The encompass solution was successfully deployed in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust in November 2023. The deployment is currently in a Stabilisation phase.It is not unexpected for programmes of this size and scale to experience issues during and after implementation. The issues being experienced at South Eastern Trust are not unique to Northern Ireland and the encompass programme is drawing on the experience of counterparts in the UK and worldwide, to put in place support systems and processes to identify and resolve issues as they occur.  These mitigations include;A weekly encompass Stabilisation Group incorporating members from the Trust, Epic and encompass. Task & Finish groups to look in more detail at specific issues and provide resolution.Additional training in response to specific staff issues.The use of anonymous staff surveys before, during and after implementation that allows staff the freedom to raise queries and seek support.Sharing of the lessons learned from the South Eastern Trust implementation and Stabilisation period with the other HSC Trusts as they prepare for their own “Go-Lives”.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the spending on legal aid in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Legal aid spend as per the Legal Services Agency’s Annual Report and Accounts for each of the last five financial years is shown below:Legal Aid Spend£m2018-1984.22019-2082.12020-2175.22021-2294.72022-23101.3This is the amount spent on making legal aid payments and excludes any of the Agency’s running costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action is being taken to improve air quality in urban areas.
answer_text: The Environment Order 2002 requires district councils to declare an Air Quality Management Area where levels of pollutants exceed, or are expected to exceed objectives. Councils must then develop an action plan to improve the air quality in that area. DAERA provides financial support to the councils through the Local Air Quality Management Grant.Officials have developed a draft Clean Air Strategy for Northern Ireland, I will work with my officials and Ministerial colleagues to finalise this important cross-cutting Strategy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to devolve additional powers to local councils.
answer_text: I have no current plans to devolve any additional powers to local councils.  To do so would require a fundamental review of the current position across several Departments and would potentially require Executive referral.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when will police officers receive their backdated pay award.
answer_text: I approved the pay remit for police officers in the federated and superintending ranks on 14 March and subsequent  approval from the Department of Finance was received on 25 March 2024.  PSNI was advised the same day.  It will be for the PSNI to process and pay backdated awards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether police officers will continue to receive the Northern Ireland Transitional Allowance due to the ongoing terrorist threat.
answer_text: I can confirm that police officers continue to receive the Northern Ireland Transitional Allowance (NITA).  The payment of NITA is taken into consideration by the Police Remuneration Review Body (PRRB) during the annual police officer pay round process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any planned capital works to libraries in East Belfast.
answer_text: There are currently no planned capital works for the five public library facilities in the Belfast East constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7922/22-27, why support for childcare has not increased for student nurses in the last five years.
answer_text: In September 2022 I announced an increase of 15% to some of the means-tested elements of bursary support for nursing and midwifery students, starting from the 2022/23 academic year. This included an increase to the dependant’s allowance and parental living allowance, targeting support towards mature students with family responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to remove the early payment discount for domestic rates.
answer_text: The early payment discount will be retained for the 2024/25 rating year. Any future change to the policy would require Executive agreement. This matter was the subject of public consultation which closed on 13 February and my Department is currently reviewing the responses received on this issue to inform next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 7922/22-27, why the non-means tested bursary for student nurses has remained at the same level for the last five years, despite the rise in the cost of living.
answer_text: I have been aware of the increasing financial pressures facing students, particularly mature students with family responsibilities. In an effort to alleviate the impact of the current cost of living crisis on students on commissioned healthcare programmes, I announced in September 2022 an increase of 15% to some of the means-tested elements of bursary support for nursing and midwifery students, starting from the 2022/23 academic year.The local universities continue to receive high overall volumes of applications even though the level of pre-registration nursing and midwifery training commissioned by my Department has been increased to record levels over recent years. I will keep the bursary support provided by my Department under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she plans to change or remove the domestic rates cap.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the reply I gave to AQW 8430/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what support is provided to parents of special educational needs students, to assist with the transition from primary to secondary education.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it provides advice and guidance to schools around the P7–Year 8 transition process, as pupils’ statements of Special Educational Needs (SEN) are reviewed annually.Following receipt of the Annual Review documentation, the child's parents are issued with an Amendment Notice to the statement outlining the changes to be made and a covering letter sets out the process to be followed.  In advance of transitioning to the new school, a series of planning meetings are held involving any EA service that may be involved with the child, parents/guardians, current school and new school.  Where a profound need exists, planning may begin as early as a year in advance (end of Year 6).Parents are provided with advice around the transition process and receive a copy of their child’s transition report which is passed to the new school.  Through the planning process parents are also provided with details of the training programme to be provided to the new school to ensure continuity of the child’s support.Parents are also provided with details of the SEN Link Officer managing their child's statement, alongside details for the SEN Helpline which they can contact to get advice regarding SEN.Information for parents regarding the Annual Review process is available on the EA's website https://send.eani.org.uk/statutory-assessment/statement-sen

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she plans to bring forward similar legislation to the Building Safety Act 2022 that passed at Westminster following the Grenfell tragedy.
answer_text: Officials in my Department’s Residential Building Safety Division are currently carrying out a review of existing building safety policy and legislation within Northern Ireland. As part of the review, they are examining the statutory frameworks that are currently in place and those that are under development in other jurisdictions, which includes the Building Safety Act 2022. This review, along with other research, scoping and stakeholder engagement will inform any legislation that needs to be amended or introduced for Northern Ireland. It will be important to ensure any such legislation places resident safety throughout the life of a residential building at its core and that it is appropriate for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the staff turnover rate in the Housing Executive Belfast Housing Solutions in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows. “The table below details the staff turnover rate in Belfast Housing Solutions in each of the last three years. Staff turnover has increased over the last three years as shown below.   YearTurnover (excluding agency workers)21/224.65%22/239.57%23/248.08% The Housing Executive have placed a strong focus on recruitment over the last few years in an effort to fill vacancies. NIHE has conducted 6 recruitment exercises for Housing Advisors since January 2022 to date which have resulted in 29 appointments so far with further appointments expected in June/July 2024 from the most recent permanent recruitment exercise.  In December 2023 they also piloted open recruitment for Belfast Housing Solutions, and this has also resulted in new appointments. In addition, managers are implementing a wider range of other actions, supported by Human Resources, in order to support employees to attend work and this includes reviewing role design and workload, and work is ongoing in these areas.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the sickness absence rate in the Housing Executive Belfast Housing Solutions in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“Please see the below table detailing the sickness absence rate in Belfast Housing Solutions in each of the last 3 years. Sickness absence has increased over the last 3 years as outlined below. Turnover has impacted this, as has the difficultly in permanently filling all posts in the structure.Year%01/04/21 – 31/03/224.6%01/04/22 – 31/03/237.1%01/04/23 – 31/03/2412.3%NIHE have placed a strong focus on recruitment over the last few years in an effort to fill vacancies. They conducted 6 recruitment exercises for Housing Advisors from January 2022 to date which have resulted in 29 appointments so far with further appointments expected in June/July 2024 from the most recent permanent recruitment exercise.  In December 2023 they also piloted open recruitment for Belfast Housing Solutions and this has also resulted in new appointments. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of staff turnover rates in Belfast Housing Solutions on the Housing Executive's ability to deliver its services.
answer_text: Matters such as these are monitored via the Partnership Agreement between my Department and the NIHE. This Agreement sets out the overall governance framework within which the NIHE operates, including the framework through which the necessary assurances are provided to stakeholders. These arrangements provide me with the assurances that I require in respect of service delivery. The Chief Executive has advised me as follows.“The current environment for the recruitment and retention of skilled and experienced staff is challenging and is being felt across many organisations including the Housing Executive.In relation to the NIHE Belfast Housing Solutions Unit, available staffing resources have been reorganised to ensure that the key business priority of meeting the Housing Executive’s statutory housing and homelessness obligation is fulfilled. A consequence of this is however that there are delays in accessing other elements of Belfast Housing Solutions’ services. They recognise the impact that staffing turnover and shortfalls have had upon their customer services and on the reduced staffing team delivering these services.A Recruitment and Retention Action Plan is currently ongoing, and this includes three recent Housing Advisor recruitment exercises in addition to testing out new roles to support longer-term succession planning. In addition, former staff have been redeployed on an ancillary basis to conduct Housing Solutions interviews and assessments. A review of the action plan is ongoing, and it is a key objective to achieve the necessary staffing levels as quickly as practicable to enable the Belfast Housing Solutions Teams to restore its full range of customer services.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support his Department provides to parents of special educational needs pupils once their child leaves education.
answer_text: Health and Social Care will support a cohort of children with Special Educational Needs throughout their life in school and transition to adult services. Supports provided are dependent on assessed need, although it is likely that HSC services will have a continued presence in the lives of children with disabilities and their families. This may include day activity, transport, short breaks, accommodation based support, and self-directed support. In addition, there are children that will have enduring healthcare needs, which are met by nursing and Allied Health Professionals. The Department is aware of several longstanding challenges to plan for the ongoing care of children with disabilities as they transition to adult social care. To improve transitions, the Department established a sub-group which aims to provide a regionally standardised approach to transition planning. To date, this group has developed a draft transition protocol for children and adult services.  However, it is recognised that effective transitions management is ultimately dependent on the availability of appropriate services. My officials are currently finalising the service model for learning disabilities and a framework for children with disabilities. I will consider how we seek wider views on the content and implementation in the coming months. Beyond health and social care, meaningful transition management and support can only be achieved through cross-departmental collaboration and inter-agency working across government. The Departments of Health and Education co-chair a cross-departmental Transitions working group with relevant departments to review and improve transitions for children with Special Educational Needs. To date, the group has conducted a survey and the outputs will be used to improve the transitions process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to allow the use of the mLink mobile ticketing app for all bus and rail services.
answer_text: The decision to allow the use of the mLink mobile ticketing app for all bus and rail services is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the work undertaken with the Department of Health to ensure a smooth transition when a special educational needs pupil leaves education.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Department of Education (DE) and Department of Health (DoH) jointly chair the Transitions Working Group which specifically aims to (i) understand the experiences and perceptions of the transition planning process; (ii) focus on current processes and practices and on the information available to those going through the transition planning process, including parents and carers; and (iii) identify improvements and synergies to create better ways of working and sharing of information. The working group includes representation from the Education Authority (EA), the Public Health Authority (PHA) and from the departments for Communities (DfC) and Economy (DfE). A survey ran from 31 January to 13 March 2024 to gather the views on the transition process for young people with special educational needs (SEN) from education into adulthood.  The responses are currently being analysed and will be considered alongside the views of young people to inform the future direction of the transition planning process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 7924/22-27, whether there has been any assessment as to why there has been little movement from suppliers to enter the gas supply market since it opened to competition.
answer_text: The local natural gas market has been fully open to supply competition for several years. The Utility Regulator (UR) conducted reviews of competitiveness of the domestic gas markets in the north in 2017 and 2021 with both identifying, that due to market scale and other contributory factors there was low market activity with no recent new entrants and very low levels of switching. However, the reviews also identified that the price customers pay for gas were amongst the lowest in Europe and the UR was of the view that gas customers in the north had been well served by the current regulatory approach and market outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to introduce legislation similar to the Down Syndrome Act 2022 passed at Westminster.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to introduce legislation similar to the Down Syndrome Act 2022 in Northern Ireland. The Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (NI) Act 1978 remains the primary legislative framework which guides the provision of social care for people with learning disabilities.As part of the ongoing exercise to finalise the Learning Disability Service Model, my officials will consider potential legislative changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how drivers, who are due to travel in their vehicle outside Northern Ireland, can avail of a quicker MOT appointment before their current MOT certificate expires.
answer_text: The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to travel outside Northern Ireland is to:•	book the earliest available appointment; •	keep checking the booking system for an appointment before their MOT expires. If they have been unable to secure a suitable appointment they should complete the short notice appointment request form, and the DVA will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to boost the capacity of the online MOT booking system in order to reduce the queue time to enter the website.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) online booking system, and the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) computer network, have experienced a significant increase in internet traffic as a result of third-party applications (BOTs) accessing those systems and automatically finding and blocking appointments, which reduces the availability of appointments for other customers to book. The DVA is committed to ensuring that all its customers have a fair and equal opportunity to book a vehicle or driving test appointment.  To ensure that can be achieved, and to reduce the amount of ‘BOT’ traffic on our systems, the DVA has implemented a range of counter measures, including a queuing system. The DVA continues to work with its strategic partners to improve counter measures against BOTs and other cyber malicious software.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the delivery of a deposit return scheme.
answer_text: DAERA committed back in February 2020 to Northern Ireland’s continued participation in the development of UK-wide proposals to reform the Packaging Producer Responsibility system and the introduction of a Deposit Return Scheme. My Department published a Government Response to the 2021 Joint consultation with England and Wales in January 2023 with 83% respondents in support of introducing a DRS. The Government Response proposed to include in the regulations a commencement date for DRS of 1st October 2025 as a stretching target date. To ensure due consideration is given to all parties involved, following publication of the Government response to the last formal consultation in January 2023, officials across the UK have been working together to ensure compatibility between different DRS’s throughout the UK. Alongside this work officials have been working with their counterparts in Ireland to prevent any unintended consequences in the two schemes and consider lessons they have learned in launching their scheme which went live on February 1st, 2024. I aim to share the outcome of this engagement in a joint policy statement with the other UK nations in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what action his Department plans to take to address the litter associated with single-use disposable vapes.
answer_text: I am conscious of the growing scale of the environmental challenges arising from disposable vapes, including the propensity for them to be littered. I’m pleased to advise that new obligations for retailers selling vapes have been introduced under the UK-wide Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) Regulations, including here in Northern Ireland. It is now a legal requirement for all retailers selling vapes to provide in-store takeback for waste vapes to ensure their proper disposal.As well as health policy proposals to tackle vapes, the recent UK-wide Health-led consultation ‘Creating a smoke free generation’ published at the end of last year, also contained environmental proposals to restrict the sale and supply of disposable vapes, due to their environmental impact. Following the publication of the joint Government response to the consultation in January this is year, I am considering my position in relation to disposable vapes, including a potential ban, and will set out the next steps I propose to take in the coming weeks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the publication of a litter strategy.
answer_text: The draft Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP), which is subject to approval by the Executive, includes a commitment to publish Northern Ireland’s first overarching Litter Strategy by 2027. My officials have already commenced initial preparatory work on the draft Litter Strategy, which will be subject to public consultation at the appropriate juncture.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on capital projects currently planned for the East Belfast constituency.
answer_text: Details of the planned works in Belfast for the 2023/24 financial year are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Belfast City Council which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme of proposed works for 2024/25 will be developed once future budgets are agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what means of support or assistance his Department provides for those on Universal Credit and other benefits in relation to broadband provision, internet connection and computer hardware.
answer_text: My Department’s 35 Jobs & Benefit offices (JBo) each have dedicated digital zones where people can access help if they don’t have access to the internet or a digital device or if they need help to get online. Staff across the JBO network also provide information on:The Department’s Adviser Discretion Fund (ADF) - a non-repayable grant award of up to £1500 (within a 12 month period). The grant may be used to buy goods or services to remove a barrier to employment, including discounted broadband or to purchase computer hardware to benefit claimants preparing, starting or returning to work; and Discounted home broadband packages, known as Social Tariffs, that are offered by a range of providers to households in receipt of benefits.Additionally, JBo staff partner with the NICS “Go ON NI” team to help claimants with digital skills. My Department also funds access to free WI-FI, computers, and support with online information and services through the local Libraries network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the location of any Invest NI land in East Belfast.
answer_text: Invest NI does not have a land holding in the East Belfast parliamentary constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the roll-out of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031.
answer_text: Delivery of the Mental Health Strategy has been taking place within an extremely challenging financial context using existing, limited resources to take forward actions. As a result of this, the Strategy is being implemented through Annual Delivery Plans which set year-on-year priorities and actions. Good progress has been made in taking forward a number of actions, including:Implementation of the community perinatal mental health service across the region, with teams now in place in all Trusts;Co-design of a three-year implementation plan for the establishment of the Regional Mental Health Service (RMHS); Completion of a three-year Early Intervention and Prevention Action Plan, with progress made on implementation of actions;Completion and publication of an independent Mental Health workforce review, with work underway to begin planning implementation of key recommendations;   Additional investment in Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) to enhance existing provision and alleviate pressures;Roll-out of the emotional health and wellbeing teams in schools, with initial cohort of schools identified and engaged;Establishment of the Digital Mental Health Board and appointment of project leads to develop a digital mental health service action plan; and  Completion of a review exploring the optimal approach to engaging the community and voluntary sector in the development of mental health policy and provision.The Annual Delivery Plan for 2024/25 is currently in development and will be co-produced through engagement with a wide range of stakeholders. This Plan will set out an agreed set of actions which will be taken forward in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to reduce waiting times for referrals for children to mental health services.
answer_text: My Department recognises that the number of children and young people awaiting Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services (CAMHS) has increased since 30th June 2019. Official statistics published by my Department on the 14th February 2024 highlighted that as of 31st December 2023 the total number of young people awaiting assessment across Northern Ireland was 2,095.All Trusts are reporting recruitment and retention issues along with more complex presentations and both of these factors are having an impact on capacity. Action 32 of The Mental Health Strategy (MHS) recognises the staffing challenges across CAMHS and a comprehensive workforce review was completed in March 2022. This review considered; existing workforce, the need to adopt new models of working and the importance of increasing the focus on training, recruitment and retention issues. My Department is committed in creating a workforce that will meet the needs of the population and which is fit for the future. A second implementation planning co-design workshop is planned for the 27th April, the purpose of which is to refine a workforce review implementation plan.The Mental Health Strategy acknowledged that CAMHS requires additional investment and Action 10 of MHS gives a commitment to increase funding to 10% of the Adult Mental Health Budget. My Department will continue to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts and other key stakeholders to develop services that meet not only current but emerging needs of this population and work with executive colleagues to secure the necessary resources required to meet demands.My Department oversees a Service Delivery Plan process which monitors activity and performance across all Health & Social Care Trusts (HSCT). Officials have set targets for new and review contacts within CAMHS and these are reviewed on a monthly basis. If a HSCT is underperforming against its target, my officials require explanations from them on why this is the case. When necessary HSCT’s are required to submit a service, improvement plan that details what steps they will take to improve performance against set targets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action her Department is taking to improve online safety.
answer_text: Whilst telecommunications legislation (including the regulation of social media platforms) is a reserved matter, my Department has sought to take a proactive approach towards supporting online safety.  The Online Safety Act 2023 establishes a new regulatory regime to address illegal and harmful content online.  It imposes legal requirements on providers of internet services.  Services within the scope of the Act will have to follow certain rules, including protecting users from illegal content and activity online, as well as protecting children from harmful content. I engaged on numerous occasions as the then Online Safety Bill progressed through Westminster and urged further strengthening of the proposed regime.  In particular, I set out my concerns that the lack of accountability of anonymous account holders is a key issue when seeking to prevent and detect online harms and offences.In Northern Ireland there are a range of offences that can be used to tackle and prosecute offensive online behaviour including the Protection from Harassment (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, the Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 and the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act.  No legislative gaps have been identified at this point, in terms of legislation within the Department’s remit. However, a criminal justice response alone cannot improve online safety.  We must take a holistic approach that is focussed on prevention and education, as set out in the cross Executive Online Safety Strategy and Action Plan which has been put in place to assist children and young people to participate in the online world in a positive, safe and responsible way.  The Online Safety Strategy – which is co-ordinated by the Department of Health and which involves my Department and the Department of Education amongst others – aims to educate, support and involve a wide range of key stakeholders, including, parents, carers and the wider family as well as schools, colleagues and youth organisations and practitioners working with children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what engagement he has had with Spirit Aerosystems on the impact a potential acquisition by Boeing will have on its Belfast operations.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans there are to review and update the Highway Code in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As road safety is a fully devolved matter, the Highway Code for Northern Ireland is the official guide for using our roads safely and legally. The code is periodically updated to take account of changes in legislation or new developments in the field of road safety.I am aware that a number of changes were made to the Highway Code for Great Britain in January 2022.  Those changes to the Highway Code are currently being assessed as to whether they will improve road safety here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to (i) increase; and (ii) speed up the roll-out of the use of diabetes technology.
answer_text: Within the context of the Diabetes Strategic Framework, the NI Diabetes Network sets the planning priorities for diabetes treatment and care provision in line with NICE guidelines. This has enabled development and progress to improve outcomes for People Living with Diabetes through regional service provision across a number of areas. The Regional Insulin Pumps Service, co-produced with patients, was established with quality of life a key consideration. The service continues to monitor demand and capacity regionally, considering continual technological advancements and workforce capacity. In December 2023, NICE published guidance on Hybrid closed loop systems for Type 1 Diabetes. This has been endorsed, following review, by the Department. Work is being undertaken to understand the implications of this guidance, required commissioning arrangements for NI and implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his Department is ensuring that universities and colleges are offering courses that prepare students to join the cybersecurity sector.
answer_text: My Department ensures that the further education colleges align to priority sectors when planning their higher education provision. Cybersecurity is one of these priority sectors and FE colleges provide a range of courses in this area.For instance, the CyberFirst Schools and Colleges programme, a partnership between the National Cyber Security Centre and Belfast Metropolitan College, is a two-year partnership to support young people to pursue careers in cybersecurity.Higher Level Apprenticeships (HLAs) specific to the cybersecurity industry are also available at the South Eastern Regional College, Belfast Metropolitan College and the Southern Regional College.  Other more general ICT HLAs contain modules relating to cybersecurity.  HLAs are employer led and the Department assesses any additional proposals in cybersecurity brought forward for delivery by training providers.	The universities are autonomous institutions responsible for their own course provision.  However, they offer a range of courses relevant to the cybersecurity sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what engagement his Department has had with local industry to ensure that courses and apprenticeships offered at regional colleges align with industry need.
answer_text: My Department has established a network of employer-led Sectoral Partnerships which are supported by the Further Education colleges and help ensure that the content of Traineeships, Apprenticeships and Higher-Level Apprenticeships meet the current and future needs of industry.As Non-Departmental Public Bodies, the Further Education colleges have the autonomy to set their own curriculum.  In doing so, all colleges endeavour to ensure the curriculum is responsive to the needs and demands of employers as a result of engagement with industry and other key stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed Sydenham park and ride.
answer_text: Whilst Tillysburn Park & Ride, Sydenham, was initially considered as a mitigation measure associated with the proposed York Street Interchange scheme, my Department now plans to progress the Tillysburn Park & Ride as a stand alone project in advance of the York Street Interchange scheme.The proposed Tillysburn Park & Ride site is located on the A2 Belfast to Bangor transport corridor, a key arterial route into Belfast.  It will encourage commuters to choose public transport or cycling for part of their journey.  It will also integrate with a proposed new cycle route linking Victoria Park and the Coastal path known as the Sydenham Greenway.  A Pre-Application Discussion submission for Tillysburn Park & Ride was made to Belfast City Council Planning in August 2021.  My Department is currently considering appropriate delivery mechanisms for Park & Ride sites, including Tillysburn and these considerations are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has assessed the potential costs of delivering a dedicated rail halt at the George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR), is considering how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation.Enhancing the integration of rail with other transport modes is one of 13 objectives within the draft Review. In support of this objective, the draft Review recommends improving existing rail-airport connections at George Best Belfast City Airport. Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. However, prior to implementing any recommendations, including those in relation to rail connectivity to George Best Belfast City Airport, further work will be required to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions. I can confirm that Translink has commenced a Feasibility Study which considers options for accessibility and connectivity to the City Airport. The study includes engagement with both Belfast City Airport and my Department’s Roads Service to develop options for future connections to the airport including the projected costs for each identified option. The feasibility study is due to be completed by the end of 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to review the High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The High Hedges Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 continues to provide a mechanism by which complainants can resolve disputes over high evergreen hedges, through their local Council. I have no plans to review it at present.I am happy to consider any significant issues raised regarding the application of the Act.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the roll-out of electric smart meters.
answer_text: The Department completed a cost benefit analysis of smart meters (CBA) in early 2023. The CBA report concluded that the case for the implementation of electricity smart meters had produced a significant level of benefit for consumers. The Department has commenced planning the implementation of electricity smart meters and has recently commissioned expert advice to provide a report which will detail: 1. Lessons learned from smart meter roll-outs in other jurisdictions. 2. Design principles suitable for the North of Ireland’s market and consumer needs. 3. Stakeholder engagement findings. Following the completion of the report the Department will consult on the design principles for a roll-out.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department is taking to increase competition in the gas supply market.
answer_text: The local natural gas market has been fully open to supply competition for several years and a number of companies are active in supplying the business sector.  Where the incumbent suppliers, SSE Airtricity and Firmus Energy, retain a dominant market share in the domestic and low consumption business sectors in their respective gas licence areas, they are subject to a regulated tariff regime. As part of the tariff review process, which is carried out in liaison with the Department and the Consumer Council, the Utility Regulator rigorously scrutinises every element of the gas tariff to ensure it reflects the actual cost of purchasing and supplying gas to homes and small businesses.   This ensures that gas consumers do not pay any more than necessary for their gas.
