question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will increase the frequency of roadside (i) grass cutting; and (ii) weed spraying.
answer_text: My Department is committed to protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges and our approach aims to maintain the right balance between road safety and the control of grass and weeds, while achieving a greater focus on environmental protection. With that in mind, in October 2022, I announced a new approach to verge management across the road network to protect the environment.From the 2023/24 year, a single swathe is being cut along the verges on the strategic road network twice per year. Other areas that are needed for road safety purposes, such as for sight lines at junctions, are also being cut at least twice each year. A similar approach has been introduced on heavier trafficked rural roads; and on lighter trafficked rural roads, where possible, the cutting frequency is being reduced to once per year and towards the end of the growing season.The new approach increases the Department’s focus on protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges and will support the actions needed to comply with the Climate Change Act passed by the Assembly in 2022.The level of grass cutting and weed spraying that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Belfast Region City Deal.
answer_text: The Belfast Region City Deal was signed in December 2021 and is now in delivery phase. Funding is being drawn down for the most advanced projects. Legally binding contracts for funding have been signed for eight projects, with a further five expected in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the Gilder service since its introduction.
answer_text: I am delighted to state that the Glider service has been a major Public Transport success story since its introduction in 2018. It has provided an extra 45,000 additional passenger journeys per week and customer feedback has highlighted its frequency, punctuality and passenger comfort as major factors when choosing how to travel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has any plans to relocate or rationalise any departmental estates.
answer_text: ANSWERA Review of the Department of Finance Office Estate was agreed in 2022 and will rationalise the office estate with the aim of reducing its in-scope footprint by 40% by 2028. My Department is focussed on maximising the utilisation of our assets and works closely with departments occupying our premises to seek to achieve this. At present the plans will see a consolidation of office accommodation within the existing locations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what schemes in the East Belfast constituency will benefit from his recent £8.1 million funding announcement for structural road maintenance.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. However, the development of the programmes is on-going so full breakdown of these is not yet available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the economic impact of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021.
answer_text: The first report on the review of the implementation and effectiveness of the Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 is expected to be published in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail planned capital spending to improve facilities at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust have commenced a project to build an Urgent Care Centre co-located with the new Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital at an estimated cost of £4m.In addition, the Trust has advised there are several projects in the business case pipeline including an upgrade of the paediatric theatres and the refurbishment of elements of the hospital site, such as outpatients and the multi-story car park. The progression of these projects will be dependent on capital budget affordability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has plans to review regulations regarding letting agents.
answer_text: There is currently no regulation of letting agents in Northern Ireland. However, this is being considered by my Department as part of Phase 2 of Private Rented Sector reform, and research has already been commissioned to help inform thinking.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions are being taken to speed up the response of statutory agencies to planning applications.
answer_text: My Department has taken a number of steps to speed up the response of statutory consultees to planning applications through the ongoing work of the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum which links statutory consultees with local planning authorities.Progress achieved includes the development of quarterly and annual reports on statutory consultation performance; a review of statutory consultee standing advice and published guidance; production of a protocol for statutory consultees which sets out what involvement/support is required from them to support an effective and efficient planning system; and the delivery of a comprehensive training programme on Environmental Impact Assessments.Actions through the Planning Improvement Programme, including the introduction of statutory validation checklists, will help improve the quality of planning applications submitted at the outset. However due to years of underfunding, statutory consultees in DfI and other departments are not sufficiently staffed or resourced to take forward the volume of planning consultations issued to them. It is important that the whole planning system is properly resourced and staffed in order to improve its efficiency and effectiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current average waiting times at the Emergency Department at the Ulster Hospital; and (ii) his plans to address this figure.
answer_text: Urgent and emergency care data for clinical care episodes completed on or after 9 November 2023 in SEHSCT is not currently available due to the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records. Therefore, SEHSCT data has been excluded from all Department Type and Northern Ireland level figures published at this time. Despite waiting times data not being available, my Department has taken a number of steps to improve patient flow at the Ulster Hospital, as the Department recognises the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the emergency department. Following a review of Emergency Medicine in NI, my Department is taking forward a number of actions to improve pressures on the wider unscheduled care system to reduce overcrowding and delays in the emergency department.  This includes actions to reduce ambulance delays, improve patient flow through emergency departments and the wider hospital system, and to ensure timely discharge for patients who no longer require hospital care.  The South Eastern HSC Trust has been working with the Ambulance Service to put in place alternative pathways for patients to access a range of services as alternatives to attending an emergency department, where this is appropriate.  A significant programme of work has been undertaken to provide alternative, appropriate services for people with Urgent Care Centres opened at Lagan Valley and the Downe Hospitals.  At the Ulster Hospital a Minor Injury Unit was opened in 2023 to provide urgent care to patients not requiring attendance at the emergency department.  This aims to ensure that emergency services are available for those who need them. Monthly performance meetings to review 4-hour metrics and the number of patients waiting more than 12 hours in the emergency department have been held with the South Eastern HSC Trust to monitor patient delays, prior to the implementation of the new digitalised health records system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will bring forward legislation to control the sale of vapes.
answer_text: Whilst vaping may have a role to play in smoking cessation, I share the growing concerns about escalating recreational use of vaping devices by young people. I have recently expressed my support for NI inclusion in the planned UK Government Tobacco and Vapes Bill which aims to create a smoke free generation and to tackle youth vaping. The NI Executive have agreed that a Legislative Consent Motion is progressed to allow for NI inclusion in the Government Bill. I have written to UK Government on that basis, and the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care (The Rt Hon Victoria Atkins) has now confirmed that Northern Ireland will be included in the Bill subject to Assembly agreement. Alongside the smokefree generation proposals to ban sales of tobacco to anyone born after the 1 January 2009, the Bill (if passed) will also provide us with a number of powers to allow for regulations to be brought before the NI Assembly in order to address the uptake in youth vaping.The UK Government also plans to restrict the sale of disposable vapes. It is anticipated that this will be progressed under environmental legislation in Great Britain. The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs is considering next steps for Northern Ireland.A new Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland is in the early stages of development, and it is expected that this will set strategic direction in relation to e-cigarettes and other emerging nicotine products.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to review the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (“the 2011 Act”) is a key piece of legislation which provides the foundation upon which statutory bodies, including local councils, the PSNI and my Department, carry out their duties in order to improve animal welfare.It is a robust piece of legislation which provides a range of powers for dealing with animal welfare cases. These include being proactive in dealing with cases in order to prevent unnecessary suffering and tough fines and penalties for serious animal welfare offences. The 2011 Act is on a par with animal welfare legislation in the rest of the United Kingdom.  Therefore, I currently have no plans to review the 2011 Act. I would nevertheless consider review as and when evidence is presented for change.The suitability and capability of wider Animal Welfare legislation is however something I am currently reviewing with officials.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 6690/22-27, to detail the actions being taken to recover this lost revenue.
answer_text: Land & Property Services (LPS) has robust billing, collection and recovery processes in place to maximise rate collection and reduce rating debt.  These processes include issuing annual rate bills and final demands for payment.  Where payment or arrangements to pay are not made, LPS will initiate recovery procedures, this includes formal written correspondence, e-mails and direct telephone contact with ratepayers.LPS will progress legal recovery actions for persistent non-payment. This includes progression through the Magistrate Courts, the Enforcement of Judgement’s Office and initiation of bankruptcy proceeding. Ratepayers experiencing financial difficulties are encouraged to contact LPS to discuss payment options which can include restructuring current payment plans or establishing longer term payment arrangements to enable payment of rate arrears.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to bring forward legislation on the minimum unit pricing of alcohol.
answer_text: The harm from alcohol consumption is a key issue in Northern Ireland, and under the NI Executive’s substance use strategy Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery, I will continue to work across government and with all key stakeholders to improve our treatment and support services and to reduce the harm it causes.A public consultation was held on alcohol pricing policies to reduce alcohol related harm, including the potential introduction of Minimum Unit Pricing for Alcohol, in NI during 2022. Modelling showing the impact of introducing this measure here was also updated at this time.I will consider the potential follow-up policy options and set out a way forward to be considered by the Executive in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the recommendations of the Sentencing Policy Review.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have continued to work on the detail of the recommendations of the Sentencing Policy Review during the absence of the Assembly and have been monitoring sentencing policy developments elsewhere in the UK since the Way Forward report was published in 2021.  It remains my intention to bring forward a sentencing bill during the remainder of this mandate which will create the legislative basis for the implementation of the key recommendations of the Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will increase student nurse bursaries to reflect the increase in the cost of living.
answer_text: The bursary arrangements for healthcare students are kept under continuous review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department inspects council household recycling centres to ensure that they meet public need.
answer_text: My Department issues a waste management licence (WML) to Local Councils for each Household Waste Recycling Centre that they operate and manage. These WML’s contain conditions to ensure the activities authorised do not cause pollution of the environment or harm to human health and specific, risk based inspections are carried out by Departmental officials to ensure compliance with these conditions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the roll-out of contactless payments on Ulsterbus services.
answer_text: Translink has implemented contactless payments on approximately 40% of their Ulsterbus vehicles, and the system is scheduled to be rolled-out to the remaining vehicles throughout this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Sydenham bypass widening scheme.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety. Work on the proposed on the A2 Sydenham Bypass widening scheme is currently paused.Details of the current status of the scheme will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: A2 Sydenham Bypass - overview | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to reduce parking queues at the Royal Victoria Hospital during visiting times.
answer_text: The aim of the Department’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine to enable them to take into account local issues. The Royal Victoria Hospital site has approximately 2700 car parking spaces and capacity is a significant and ongoing challenge for the Belfast HSCT to manage. The Trust has no current plans to increase capacity, however, there are a number of alternative plans and projects underway  that will assist in reducing the demand on patient and visitor car parks. These projects include an upgrade to the current car park barrier system in April 2024 to help manage capacity and traffic flow on the Royal Victoria Hospital site, installation of digital signage in March 2024 to assist bus users with timetables and next bus arrival times at the free park and ride facility at Blacks Road, and the placement of parking attendants at car park entrances to direct users to alternative car parks with capacity.In addition, to help reduce the demand on patient and visitor car parks, the Trust has increased the number of cycle shelters and showering facilities on the site to encourage cycling, running or walking to work, and the Trust will be launching an electric car pool pilot scheme for staff in April 2024. The Trust is also working collaboratively with government departments to provide viable alternatives to single occupancy car use and to increase off-site car parking capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the roll-out of LED street lighting in East Belfast.
answer_text: Unfortunately, my Department is unable to provide this information by parliamentary constituency area.  However, I can confirm that in the Belfast City Council area, which includes the East Belfast constituency area, as of 26 February 2024, there are a total of 45,522 street lights of which 32,383 (71%) are LED lanterns.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department carries out traffic and parking assessments for planning applications.
answer_text: As a consultee, my Department provides specialist safety information and transportation advice to the appropriate Planning Authority on road related matters associated with proposed development applications. In assessing such applications, officials are informed by current planning policy, development plans and supplementary planning guidance before concluding a recommendation to the Planning Authority.  It is the responsibility of the applicant to provide sufficient information within their application to adequately demonstrate that the development proposal satisfies current policy, design standards, and other relevant supplementary planning guidance in respect of traffic impact and parking.  Where necessary, my officials review and re-assess the information provided by applicants to validate the acceptability of the information provided, including the proposed level and arrangement of parking provision and any proposed measures to mitigate the traffic impacts of the development proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether there are any plans to regulate management companies in private developments to ensure that service charges are fair to residents.
answer_text: I will engage with officials within the Department to assess how this issue can be addressed. Future work will be dependent on competing priorities and available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what progress has been made in reducing the use of off-contract agency staff across health and social care.
answer_text: Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust expenditure on agency staff is incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained.  The use of agency staff in HSC, therefore, contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients.In October 2022, as the Health Minister I stated that “off contract” expenditure is not a cost-effective use of taxpayers’ money, and can lead to a lack of workforce continuity, with the potential to undermine safe care.Since then work has been ongoing regionally across HSC towards the cessation of ‘Off-Framework’ agency use, initially within Nursing and Midwifery and Social Workers. Work has also been focused on Recruitment, Retention and Training of staff, in addition to, a dedicated workstream examining the reform of the Bank system. In August 2023, a new Framework for the supply of agency nurses, and midwives meant that HSC Trusts were no longer able to use any ‘off’ contract agencies, where no contracted terms are in place.I very much welcome the ongoing progress that has been made across HSC in relation to reducing agency usage especially at a time when the health service is facing a period of unprecedented budgetary pressures. Reducing agency spend is important and not just because of the costs involved; HSC staff have been clear that excessive agency use is unfair and impacts on morale and more importantly, having consistent clinical teams also enhances patient safety.As a result of this work there is projected to be an overall net reduction in agency spend in 23/24 in the region of £20m.  It should be noted these figures are estimates and should be treated with extreme caution at this time.  Transformation of health and social care in Northern Ireland is an essential priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there are any requirements placed on local councils with regards to location and capacity of household recycling centres.
answer_text: Article 25 of the Waste and Contaminated Land Order (Northern Ireland) 1997 provides detail for councils on the operation and location of household recycling centres including provisions that state a ‘district council may provide within or outside its district—places at which to deposit waste before the council transfers it to a place or plant or equipment…’. Paragraph 4 also states that these places ‘shall either be situated within the district of the council or, if not so situated, be reasonably accessible to persons resident in that district’.Whilst there are no specific legislative requirements for capacity, a key condition of each recycling centre waste management licence is that the operator take appropriate measures to ensure that compliance with the waste hierarchy referred to in Article 4 of the Waste Framework Directive is applied to the generation of waste by the activities. In addition, a summary record of the waste types accepted and removed from the site is required to be made for each quarter of the financial year and submitted to the Department.The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates a total of 94 household recycling centres across Northern Ireland. NIEA staff carry out site inspections on a regular basis to check each site is compliant with the conditions of its waste management licence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current applications for the funding of new school buildings in East Belfast.
answer_text: Elmgrove Primary School is the only current ongoing major capital project in the East Belfast constituency.This school was announced to proceed to planning in January 2013.  Woodvale Construction commenced construction work on site in March 2021 and the current estimated completion date is January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many new Nurture groups have been announced by his Department in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department of Education has announced 31 new Nurture Groups in the last five years, bringing the total number of Nurture Groups funded by the Department to 62.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is considering significant reform, or removal, of points awarded due to intimidation in reviewing the Housing Executive housing points allocation system.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing in relation to the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations. My officials are working with the Housing Executive to consider the way forward for the proposal relating to intimidation points. This includes independent research which is due to be complete by the end of March 2024.Once options in relation to this proposal have been further developed, I will give them my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on Reshaping Stroke Care.
answer_text: While capacity and funding constraints have impacted the pace of delivery, progress has nonetheless been made against a range of the priorities set out in the Action Plan. This includes the development of a regional model for TIA (mini-stroke); the completion of a Thrombolysis audit; the introduction of Artificial Intelligence software in five hospitals to assist in the interpretation of scans; and the development of a specification for rehabilitation and long-term support.    Work is ongoing on other key priorities. This includes Thrombectomy where a costed model identifying requirements to support expansion has been developed. In addition, a revised process to support the identification of a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care has been agreed, with preparatory work already underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure which schools in East Belfast will be included in the 20mph speed limit zoning for the next financial year.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: Road safety at schools RSPPG E070 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk)The program to date has been rolled out in tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail plans to improve staff parking at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The aim of the Department’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine to enable them to take into account local issues. The Ulster Hospital has approximately 2200 parking spaces of which approximately 1300 are designated for permitted staff parking. Capacity is constrained by the geography of the site, planning restrictions and the number of entrances and exits serving the site.  There is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with an on-site car parking space, however the trust provides over 100 free off-site staff parking spaces at Dundonald Omniplex with a courtesy shuttlebus  operating every 15 minutes at peak times and every 30 minutes at all other times.  The courtesy bus and off-site car park operating times, capacity and frequency are constantly under review.The trust realises that car parking is a genuine concern for their staff and is working collaboratively with government departments to provide viable alternatives to single occupancy car use and to increase off-site staff car parking capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many times utility providers have been instructed by his Department to make good a road surface, following poor resurfacing, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Utility companies are responsible for the permanent reinstatement of roads to an agreed specification. When utility companies do not reinstate roads in a satisfactory manner, the Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections. The number of reinstatement failures is set out in the table below. YearReinstatement Failures2019/20201,0242020/20217592021/202210092022/202312592023/2024 (as at 19 February)824The reinstatement defects recorded in the table were notified to the company responsible for the necessary remedial works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what action is being taken to increase the availability of emergency housing in Belfast.
answer_text: The increasing of availability of emergency housing in Belfast is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on works on each of the tower blocks in East Belfast.
answer_text: Works on each of the tower blocks in East Belfast is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Living Over the Shops Scheme.
answer_text: The Department previously funded a NIHE pilot Living Over The Shops (LOTS) scheme from 2002 – 2009. The scheme closed to new applications in 2009 due to budgetary constraints and because it did not deliver on anticipated outcomes.An independent review of the Living Over the Shops scheme was carried out in 2016, a copy of the report can be accessed online at the following link - Review of The Living Over The Shops Scheme - Final Report (September 2016)This Review identified numerous issues with creating homes in vacant spaces above retail units which the provision of a small grants scheme did not significantly overcome.  It also noted that the Scheme had not significantly contributed to housing supply or regeneration. The Department consulted on a new Housing Supply Strategy in 2021/22. It is intended that this Strategy will continue work to make the best use of existing assets and include consideration of actions around housing led regeneration and use of existing buildings to generate additional supply. I am currently reviewing this Strategy and considering options for progressing it.

answer_text: ANSWERThe table below provides the End of Year rating debt value for the rating years ended 31st March 2019 to 31st March 2022, broken down by Domestic and Non-Domestic. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of Housing Executive properties in East Belfast that are void; and (ii) what actions are being taken to reduce that number.
answer_text: The number of Housing Executive properties in East Belfast that are void is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there are plans to review court witness expense allowances, to reflect actual costs incurred.
answer_text: The setting and payment of court witness expense allowances is a matter for the Public Prosecution Service (PPS). Any decision to review these expenses is also a policy matter for the PPS. You may therefore wish to direct your question directly to the PPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to extend the Gilder service in Dundonald beyond the park and ride.
answer_text: There are no plans to extend the Gilder service in Dundonald beyond the park and ride at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the rehousing of tenants in the Kinross Avenue flats, Tullycarnet, that are due for demolition this year.
answer_text: The rehousing of tenants in the Kinross Avenue flats, Tullycarnet, is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what support his Department will give local councils to provide new or upgraded household recycling centres that cater for modern recycling separation requirements.
answer_text: My Department’s £23m Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP) assists local councils to transform kerbside recycling and Household Recycling Centre infrastructure and services to help deliver a circular economy in Northern Ireland. This is achieved by supporting capital projects that improve both the quantity and quality of household waste recycling collected. Reducing the quantity of residual waste will also reduce reliance on landfill and recovery. Funding of up to 50% eligible costs is available. To date, £4.9m has been made available for nine projects including upgrades to four Household Recycling Centres at Magherafelt, Sullatober, Gortrush and Drummee. Allocations of funding under the Programme must be made by 31 March 2026.Following the completion of a review of the HWRCCP, my officials have recently written to all council Chief Executives and Directors of Environmental Services advising them of revised HWRCCP criteria and arrangements for submitting applications.  Further information on the fund and application process can be found on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the implementation of the 10x Economy strategy.
answer_text: The 10x Economic Vision delivery plan was published in July 2023, which set out a range of actions being taken by my Department to deliver against the 10x objectives.  I have now set out how my four economic priorities will shape the activity of the Department for the remainder of this mandate. These reflect the fact that since 10x was published we have had the Lyons Review of Invest NI and now have certainty around our trading conditions. Work that is ongoing that will contribute to my priorities will continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of applicants on the housing waiting list in East Belfast, broken down by number of bedrooms required, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Details of the number of applicants on the social housing waiting list in East Belfast broken down by the number of bedrooms required is operational information which the Housing Executive holds.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many Young for Year children have deferred the start of pre-school since The School Age (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 was introduced.
answer_text: Since The School Age (Northern Ireland) Act was introduced into law in April 2022 the parents of 364 Young for Year children have chosen to defer the start of their child’s pre-school education. This includes children who have deferred the 2024/25 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the demolition of Kilbroney House, Belfast.
answer_text: The demolition of Kilbroney House, Belfast is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should, therefore, direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to improving the Grahamsbridge Road, Comber Road and Old Dundonald Road junction, Dundonald.
answer_text: The Department had previously commissioned a study to consider a number of possible options to improve the junction including the provision of traffic signals. The study examined signalising the junction to varying degrees including some or all of the vehicle movements.The findings concluded that a major reconstruction of the layout of this junction would be required to appropriately deal with the capacity issues that exist at this location.  Given the funding constraints currently faced by my Department, improvements at this junction, in comparison to other similar junctions, have not been considered to rank sufficiently high to be included on a current works programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of the housing points allocation system.
answer_text: Implementation of eighteen of the twenty proposals to change the Housing Selection Scheme is ongoing. It is being carried out on a four-phase approach by the Housing Executive with support and oversight from officials within my Department. Stage One was implemented on 31 January 2023. Stage Two, containing the next five proposals, is almost complete. I will provide an update on this stage in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the increase in demand to the Arts Council’s Musical Instruments Programme., whether he will consider other ways to improve the (i) funding; and (ii) support provided for marching bands.
answer_text: The Bands scheme was the first to be introduced in the Arts Council’s Musical Instruments Programme.  It is there in recognition of the important tradition of Banding in communities across Northern Ireland and the role bands can play in providing young people with the opportunity to learn to play and to develop their musicianship.The Musical Instruments Programme is competitive and the number of awards is limited by the amount of funding available. In 2023/24 the budget for the whole programme was set at £400k and half of that was awarded through the Bands scheme.The Budget for 2024-25 has not been settled.  Future funding of this programme, along with all the other valuable calls for investment, will be considered in the light of decisions on the budget.I am open to ideas and proposals which are directly aimed at supporting delivery and activity across my portfolio, including in terms of Banding, but these would need to be fall within the scope of my department and its arms-length bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) how many recommendations from the report review of the implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 have been carried out to date; and (ii) when this will be completed.
answer_text: The report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 made numerous recommendations/actions covering key elements of the planning system.  Good progress has already been made, including the implementation of a planning fee inflationary uplift; the progression of work to bring forward arrangements for statutory validation checklists which will improve the quality of applications entering the system; the launch (December 2023) of a public consultation into a fundamental review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015; and a review of the internal administration of the ‘call-in’ process. Enhanced use of digital technology in the planning system has also been aided with introduction of the Regional Planning IT System (Planning Portal).I will be reviewing progress with my officials; progress is however dependent on the resources available to me within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the Interim Regional Planning Commission; and (ii) what it has achieved to date.
answer_text: An interim Regional Planning Commission (iRPC) was established in early 2023, comprised of representatives from key stakeholder groups and has met on 4 occasions. The iRPC has provided advice and support to the wider planning improvement programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) how many recommendations from the Public Accounts Committee report on Planning in Northern Ireland have been carried out; and (ii) when this will be completed.
answer_text: The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) report into Planning in the north of Ireland was published in March 2022. Due to the collapse of the Assembly, a formal Memorandum of Reply was not able to be finalised. I am working with my officials through the process of replying and shall respond formally in due course to the recommendations in the PAC report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration has been given to granting District Councils the power to vary business rates throughout their council area.
answer_text: No consideration has been given to this area within my Department. Local government finance, including the setting of district rates by District Councils, is a matter that falls within the policy and legislative competence of the Minister for Communities. I can, however, consider any proposals that the Minister may bring to the Executive in relation to this area.In February 2021 my Department put in place new legislative changes to give councils the option to strike different levels of household and business rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to keep the Dundonald park and ride passenger building open on a permanent basis.
answer_text: The provision and operation of the park and ride facility at Dundonald is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he is taking to ensure the (i) security; and (ii) continuity of energy supply.
answer_text: Energy security is a key priority for the Department. The Energy Strategy ‘A Pathway to Net Zero’ sets out my Department’s ambition to deliver an energy system that is secure, affordable and sustainable. The Department works closely with the energy industry to ensure the security and continuity of energy supply, in respect of electricity, gas and oil.  My officials meet regularly with the gas and electricity transmission system operators and the Utility Regulator to review and monitor any capacity risks. To ensure resilience of the system the Department works with energy stakeholders on emergency preparedness exercises throughout the year as well as winter preparedness plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy – The Path to Net Zero Energy, was published in December 2021 following agreement by the Executive.  It contains a commitment to publish annual Reports on the delivery of cross-Departmental actions against the Strategy, to demonstrate the progress and provide an overview of strategic developments.  Annual Action Plan Reports were published on 15th February 2022 Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2022 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk) and 1st February 2023 Energy Strategy - Path to Net Zero Energy - 2023 Action Plan Report (economy-ni.gov.uk).  The 2024 Action Plan is due to be published in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of new jobs announced for East Belfast, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: Table 1 below details the number of new jobs that were publicly announced in the East Belfast Parliamentary Constituency since April 2018.  It should be noted that ‘announced’ projects present a very limited view of total Invest NI support activity.Table 1: New Jobs Announced by Invest NI within East Belfast Parliamentary Constituency Area (2018-19 to 2022-23)Year of AnnouncementNew Jobs Announced2018-193692019-20942020-21472021-22122022-2380Total602To provide a more comprehensive picture of Invest NI support activity across the area, I am supplementing this answer with information on all projects assisted during the previous five years.   During this period (April 2018 – March 2023), Invest NI provided support to businesses that will lead to the creation of 760 new jobs within the East Belfast Parliamentary Constituency. Jobs arising from these projects will be created over the next 3 to 5 years, and will result in an increase in annual salaries of £23.29m in East Belfast.  Invest NI supported these businesses with £6.96m of employment-related assistance, which will contribute towards total planned investment of £67.36m in the area. In addition, Invest NI provided 825 offers of other developmental support to businesses in East Belfast, totalling assistance of £37.32m.  This includes financial assistance for Research and Development, skills development and support for the promotion of products and services made in the East Belfast constituency in overseas markets and will contribute towards total planned investment of £207.84m.In addition to financial assistance, Invest NI also provides wider support to all businesses through advice, assistance or guidance from our staff and through nibusinessinfo.co.uk, our online business advice portal.For the Member’s future reference, Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of financial support offered to every business on the NI Open Data online portal which can be accessed from the following link: Invest NI Open Data).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to address the lack of electric vehicle charging points in the East Belfast constituency.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible EV Charge points is a commercially led operation in the North with responsibility for the maintenance and installation of any commercially operated charge point resting solely with the charge point operator.  Current providers operating in the North include ESB, Weev, Maxol, BP Pulse and Easygo. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure.The relevant charge point operators are best placed to advise you of their plans for installation of charge points in your constituency.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). These schemes are open to Councils who provide charging infrastructure for EV owners, or potential owners, who do not have driveway access (On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme - ORCS), and for residential and commercial landlords. My Department has made match-funding available to assist local councils making an application to ORCS. Belfast City Council would be able to confirm if it has made an application to this scheme.My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force, bringing together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements. An Action Plan to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network was agreed in autumn 2022. The Action Plan can be found here: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/electric-vehicle-ev-infrastructure-action-plan

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action is being taken to increase the level of blood donations in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Blood Transfusion Service (NIBTS) has seen a significant improvement in its daily stock levels over the past 6-12 months.This improvement has been largely due to an ongoing series of targeted activities to encourage donation, such as social media promotions, paid advertising, donor rewards, working with partner organisations and running promotional events, both to attract new donors and encourage existing donors to continue to donate regularly.In addition, NIBTS is developing a Donation Strategy to help promote blood donation and maintain its current stock levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department coordinates the work carried out by utility companies on roads and footways, to ensure minimal disruption for residents and businesses.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (“the Order”) forms the legislative framework for street works activities. The Department has issued a number of codes of practice under the Order which were prepared jointly by the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) and the Department.The Order places a duty on my Department to use best endeavours in the co-ordination of street works with a parallel duty on the undertakers of these works to co-operate with the department.The Code of Practice for the Co-ordination of Street Works and Works for Road Purposes and Related Matters defines the co-ordination function, with the purpose of minimising inconvenience and to protect the street structure and the apparatus within it.The Northern Ireland Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) is a vital tool in the co-ordination and communication of all such street worksAlongside the NISRANS system, officials in my department are in daily contact with utility providers to agree traffic management plans, to discuss and co-ordinate utility works and balance the potentially conflicting interests of road users and utility customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to address flooding that impacts properties in the Lambert development, Dundonald.
answer_text: My Department has responded to recent flood events at the Lambert development. On each occasion a blocked debris screen on an undesignated watercourse to the east of the Lambert development was identified as the source of the flooding.  My officials liaised with representatives for the landowner who is responsible to inspect and maintain this screen. An assurance has been received that the landowner will enhance their inspection and maintenance regime to mitigate against future flood impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the review of Residents' Parking Schemes; (ii) to detail the findings of the review; and (iii) when the findings will be published.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes has been completed and the report will be published when I have considered the review findings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to support primary care to ensure patients can appropriately register health concerns for triage and direction.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that patients will be able to continue to access high quality, sustainable primary care services now and in the future.In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those citizens who need the most urgent attention and care. My Department has made significant funding available to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and employ additional staff to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.My Department has established a GP Access group to explore issues around accessing services and to oversee work to improve the experience for patients.  As well as developing guidance for practices in the management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency, the Group is also exploring how telephony and related technologies can be best deployed to improve experience for patients and practices alike.My Department continues its focus on strengthening our GP workforce, this includes funding the Attract, Recruit, Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme, the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme, as well as increasing the number of GP training places from 65 in 2015/16, to the current level of 121 (an increase of 86%).Additional capacity in general practice is also being provided through investment in Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses, practice-based pharmacists and the roll-out of primary care Multi-Disciplinary Teams (MDT).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to give local councils more powers to address the issue of bins left on public footways beyond their collection date.
answer_text: My Department has no plans to give local councils powers to address the issue of bins left on public footways beyond their collection date, however you may wish to engage with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) who are responsible for waste management functions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will be reviewing the intervention levels for repairing road defaults.
answer_text: My Department’s Resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department is taking to address the flooding that impacts properties on Comber Road, Dundonald, which back onto Moat Park.
answer_text: In relation to flooding associated with the Enler River, which flows through Moat Park, staff from my Department’s Rivers Directorate have met with the affected residents and local council representatives. During the most recent meeting a solution to alleviate the effects of lower return period river flooding on the referenced properties was discussed. The proposed solution is being considered by Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council and it is not for action by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any plans to amend legislation to allow the suspension of bus lanes on the Upper Newtownards Road, Dundonald, for use by private vehicles during a disruption to bus and Glider services, such as strike action.
answer_text: Bus lanes are not exclusively used by Translink buses and are available for use by bicycles, permitted taxis, motorbikes, buses operated by health and education authorities, privately operated buses and coaches and emergency services.Maintaining safe spaces for cyclists and motorcyclists, as well as providing ease of access for the emergency services is always important but even more so when traffic volumes are high.The power to suspend bus lanes resides with the Chief Constable of the PSNI as per the Road Traffic Regulation Order (NI) 1997. The Department has no plans to amend this legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the actions his Department is taking to reduce waiting times for an MOT appointment.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year.  This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use overtime to provide cover for leave and offering vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.A further vehicle examiner recruitment competition is due to launch later this month to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.  The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.  The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/guide-booking-your-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.Vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a customer can change, cancel or view their MOT appointment online is available on NI Direct at change, cancel or view MOT/vehicle test appointment.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and will open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on his plans to progress the Sub-Regional Stadia fund.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for football is an important flagship Executive commitment and I recognise the valuable contribution it can deliver for football and wider society. I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not yet been delivered. Investment in local football grounds will help to support the legacy of the Euros and I look forward to progressing this programme and delivering football facilities that are modern, safe and meet the needs of our communities.My officials are working with a range of stakeholders to develop proposals. I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many in-patients are unable to be discharged from hospital as they are awaiting a care package to be put in place, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the number of in-patients in each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust who are unable to be discharged from hospital as they are awaiting a care package to be put in place, has been provided by each HSC Trust for the position on 1st August 2024 and is detailed in the Table 1 overleaf.Table 1: Number of in-patients in each HSC Trust who are unable to be discharged from hospital as they are awaiting a care package to be put in place, for the position on 1st August 2024 HSC TrustNumber Belfast188Northern226Southern378South Eastern410Western5341 Figures include patients unable to be discharged from hospital as they are awaiting a care package either in their own home or through care home admission.2 Figures include acute, community and mental health hospitals.3 Southern HSC Trust advise that it has inpatients across 4 hospitals, namely: Craigavon Area Hospital, Daisy Hill Hospital, Lurgan Hospital and South Tyrone Hospital, and includes inpatients from Mental Health Inpatient Unit (Bluestone) on the CAH site. Figures include those waiting for a care home placement to support them on discharge. 4 Figures based on inpatients in acute hospitals.5 Figures based on discharge code C110 – No Domiciliary Care Package.Domiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.My department previously announced a £70m funding package across a range of service provision, including domiciliary care. The purpose of this was to ensure independent sector providers had the necessary funding to meet the challenges of increases to national living wage from the beginning of April. I have also established structures to consider how we move the sector to a Real Living Wage employer base and significant work is ongoing to build the case for the additional funds that will be required to deliver this.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what measures he will be taking to ensure people have access to NHS dental services.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I know that an additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the Northern Ireland Housing Executive announcement that it had agreed to release maintenance contractors from planned contracts, what discussions she has had with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to ensure contractors can be secured for overdue schemes in East Belfast, in order to minimise further delays for tenants.
answer_text: Following the release of the planned schemes contractors in August 2022, my officials were in continuous contact with the Housing Executive and were kept informed of developments. Assurances from the NIHE that it was continuing to prioritise delivery of its planned maintenance programme and was working at speed to achieve a satisfactory resolution, in the best interests of its tenants, the broader public interest, and to mitigate the effects of the resulting delays to its maintenance programme were sought at Quarterly Accountability meetings and monthly oversight meetings.I have sought an update on this matter from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, and have been advised that the Housing Executive has successfully awarded a replacement contract in August 2023.  A two-year programme of works for ten projects, which includes those in the original collapsed tender, has been agreed with the Contractor.  To date, one of the projects has been completed and a further six have been briefed and are progressing through the pre-site approval process.  All works will be completed before September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has plans to provide additional financial support to social work trainees in light of the cost of living crisis.

Social Work students can access a non-means tested payment of £4,000 for each year of their study. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what plans she has to bring empty homes back into use.
answer_text: The Empty Homes Strategy and Action Plan was operational from 2013 to 2018 with a review of the Strategy completed and published on the Department’s website in 2021. The Review can be accessed at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-empty-homes-strategy-and-action-plan-2013-%E2%80%93-2018		While the Review recognised that the Strategy had limited success in addressing housing stress, as a result of this work we are now much better informed about the complexities of this issue and the reasons why so many properties appear to be vacant.		My Department has developed a draft Housing Supply Strategy which aims to address the wider challenges facing housing supply. This includes consideration of how we can make best use of existing assets and also ensuring we protect existing homes to retain them in the supply system for longer.  I am currently reviewing the Strategy with the aim of bringing a finalised version to the Executive for its consideration and endorsement in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the legal aid received by individual legal firms, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The legal aid received by individual legal firms in the years 2017/18 to 2021/22 is not currently available.  However, it is my intention to publish this information in due course.The Legal Services Agency NI (LSANI) last published earnings of the Top 100 solicitors and barristers for the year 2014/15, which is available on the Agency’s website.  Prior to publication of the information for 2015/16, two barristers brought a judicial review over the inclusion of individual’s names which delayed publication.  By the time the case came to be heard, the General Data Protection Regulations were about to come into operation.It was agreed that the Agency would not publish the information pending review of the legal challenge and consulting on the basis of publication under GDPR.During 2020/21, the Agency launched a consultation on its release of future payments information.  Consistent with briefing provided to the Justice Committee on the responses to the consultation exercise, it remains my intention to publish information on annual payments to suppliers of legal aid.  Following my direction, officials are exploring how best to facilitate this process.Current intentions are to release information spanning the years 2015/16 to 2018/19 in the same format as that adopted up to and including 2014/15.  This will include the names and total annual payments made to the Top 100 solicitor firms and barristers.  This information is in the process of being reconciled with a view to triggering the process for publication before year end, in March 2023. As this information includes the publication of personal data there are a range of steps to be taken and the variable responses to these steps will dictate the timescale of publication. As such it is not possible at this time to say when the information will be sanctioned for public release.A separate approach is needed to compile and reconcile equivalent data covering the 2019/20 year.  This year has an extra layer of complexity as a new legal aid case management system (LAMS) was introduced in July 2019, which has resulted in data for 2019/20 spanning multiple systems.  A methodology has to be finalised as to how these data can best be matched, compiled, disentangled and de-duplicated.  It is envisaged that the process to trigger release of 2019/20 will commence by September 2023.  As before, it is not possible to determine when this process will conclude to enable public release of the information which will be in the same format as previous years.Once publications of the years 2015/16 to 2019/20 have completed, the cycle for 2020/21 will commence.  As the information from 2020/21 onwards will be based solely on the new LAMS case management system, this will require a further methodological iteration to be in place to produce more granular outputs indicating overall earnings by area of legal aid expenditure.
