question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what work has been undertaken to identify and map the alleys and open spaces that are in the Department's ownership, in residential communities in Belfast.
answer_text: As Statutory Roads Authority, my Department retains adoption certificates for roads and alleyways for which the public has right of way and which are maintained by my Department. However, not all roads or alleyways have been put forward for adoption by landowners/ developers. My Department usually does not own land within residential communities.Some of the information on areas of land or public road controlled by my Department is in digital format. In 2020, the Department of Finance (DoF) started to roll out the Government Land and Property Asset Register (GLPR) programme, which will create a central register of all land and property assets owned or controlled by Government departments. My Department continues to share details of the its lands databases with the DoF project team. Officials from my Department will continue to progress this work as available resources permit, although it is acknowledged this is a significant body of work that will take some time to complete.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement has been undertaken to date with other agencies in order to address problems with alleyways across Belfast.
answer_text: I fully support local community efforts to improve the appearance of back entries and other similar spaces within their areas with the objective being to enhance community pride, reduce anti-social behaviour and improve the general area. To this end my Department launched a new alleyway transformation initiative and wrote to all Councils inviting them to scope the opportunities that exist for interventions of this nature and to apply to my Department for funding. In January 2021, funding was made available to Councils for the ‘Greening of Alleyways’.  This funding could be used either directly by the Council or be provided to communities to carry out work to improve back entries, alleyways and similar open spaces, irrespective of ownership or adoption by my Department. This funding is available irrespective of whether the site has been adopted by my Department. Officials have been engaging with Belfast City Council to develop proposals, however no funding has been allocated to date.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many liability claims have been received by the Department for injuries or damage incurred within alleyways owned by the Department.
answer_text: The Department’s Claims Management System does not record information in a format that allows the identification of alleyway specific claims.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what processes are in place to allow the Department to adopt alleyways, which have existed for long periods and require regeneration.
answer_text: It is my Department’s policy to adopt alleyways and pathways that are considered essential for safe vehicular and pedestrian access and where the area is not already served by a good network of public footways.  I fully support local community efforts to improve the appearance of back entries and other similar spaces within their areas, to enhance community pride, reduce anti-social behaviour and improve the general area. My Department launched a new alleyway transformation initiative and wrote to all Councils inviting them to scope the opportunities that exist for interventions of this nature, and to apply to my Department for funding. In January 2021, funding was made available to Councils for the ‘Greening of Alleyways’.  This funding could be used either directly by the Council, or be provided to communities to carry out work to improve back entries, alleyways and similar open spaces, irrespective of ownership or adoption by my Department. Officials have engaged with Belfast City Council (BCC) to develop proposals.  It is envisaged that the work with Councils will be progressed to the point where funding can be allocated in the second half of this financial year.  Officials have also had discussions with BCC regarding their ‘Alleyways Programme’ and to discuss the possibility of the Department lending support (by way of funding on a without prejudice basis) to this programme.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement his Department has had with Belfast City Council and other relevant agencies on improving the cleanliness of Belfast City Centre.
answer_text: When requested and where practicable, my officials have assisted local councils by providing Temporary Traffic Management (TTM) on roads to allow litter picking safely to take place. Additionally, officials from DfI Roads Belfast North Section Office do on some occasions give BCC Cleansing prior notice of planned road closures for maintenance, which provides the opportunity to carry out a clean-up during planned closures.Where BCC plans to undertake litter picking in areas where TTM is required, it should contact the local Section Engineer at BelfastNorth@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk to discuss their proposals and any requirements to create a safe working zone for their staff.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what engagement he has had with Belfast City Council and other relevant organisations regarding the removal of graffiti in Belfast City Centre.
answer_text: I have not had any direct engagement with Belfast City Council regarding the removal of graffiti in Belfast City Centre, although I am aware my officials from Roads Eastern Division had some preliminary discussions with the Probation Board some years ago on this matter.Due to resource limitations, my Department only carries out those maintenance activities which are essential to maintain the road network in a safe and serviceable condition. Graffiti is only removed where it presents a risk to road safety or is deemed offensive, racist, or sectarian in nature.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when funds, received by the Executive as Barnett consequentials, will be made available to fund the removal of unsafe non-ACM cladding from high rise buildings.
answer_text: All Barnett consequential are unhypothecated, meaning that they are not ring-fenced for specific purposes but can be allocated by the Executive in line with local needs and priorities.  The Department for Communities (DfC) and the Housing Executive are continuing to scope a non-ACM scheme. In the interests of having the best possible scheme, DfC is working with the Department for Levelling Up Housing and Communities to take account of recent developments in this policy area.  DfC will also consider how best to fund the associated costs related to the any future scheme which may include bidding for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she will be requesting funds for the remediation of unsafe non-ACM cladding in the October 2022 monitoring round process.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to scope a non-ACM scheme with support from the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the remediation scheme for unsafe non-ACM cladding will be in place.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to scope a non-ACM scheme with support from the Department of Finance.  	DfC are engaging with officials in the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities on this, with urgency.  In the meantime, details of all of the properties that were the subject of submissions to the DoF call for evidence have been shared by DoF with the Fire and Rescue Service, who have asked for additional information on buildings’ names and confirmed heights. They will consider buildings within scope and work to adapt response plans for the intention of mitigating associated risk.	Once the process of developing a suitable scheme has been agreed, the relevant information will be shared

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when she will meet with TQ Arc Building Management Company regarding the remediation scheme for unsafe non-ACM cladding.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to scope a non-ACM scheme with support from the Department of Finance.  		DfC are engaging with officials in the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities on this, with urgency.  In the meantime, details of all of the properties that were the subject of submissions to the DoF call for evidence have been shared by DoF with the Fire and Rescue Service, who have asked for additional information on buildings names and confirmed heights. They will consider buildings within scope and work to adapt response plans for the intention of mitigating associated risk.Once the process of developing a suitable scheme has been agreed, the relevant information will be shared

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people who have presented as homeless to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive due to victimisation in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) category; and (ii) area.
answer_text: The Housing Executive does not record data under the category of ‘victimisation’ however information is provided on the number of households who have presented as homeless to the Housing Executive on the basis of intimidation, broken down by category and Council Area over the 2017-22 period. Due to the large volume of data requested, tables outlining the number of households who have presented as homeless to the Housing Executive on the basis of intimidation, broken down by category and Council Area over the 2017-22 period have been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Dundela Infants School and Strandtown Primary School will be included in the next phase of the 20mph speed limit programme.
answer_text: The second tranche of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools is being delivered this year and upon completion, 215 schools across the North will be benefitting from this initiative. When all of the tranche 2 sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work to determine if a further tranche is justified. Until this review is carried out I am not in a position to commit to install part-time 20mph speed limits at any more schools, including Dundela Infants School and Strandtown Primary School.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.
